Question: A 30-year-old man with Crohn's disease has perianal disease with a complex fistula and recurrent perianal abscesses. He is currently on azathioprine for luminal disease control. Despite surgical drainage of the abscess and seton insertion, the fistula remains active. What is the most appropriate additional medical therapy?
Option_A: High-dose oral prednisolone
Option_B: Infliximab (anti-TNF therapy)
Option_C: Methotrexate as monotherapy
Option_D: Oral ciprofloxacin lifelong
Option_E: Vedolizumab (anti-integrin therapy)
Explanation: Perianal fistulising Crohn's disease is a challenging complication. NICE NG129 and ECCO guidelines recommend anti-TNF therapy (infliximab is the best-evidenced) for active perianal Crohn's fistulae that have not responded to conventional treatment (antibiotics, immunomodulators, surgical drainage). Infliximab has been shown in the ACCENT II trial to promote fistula closure and reduce the need for hospitalisation and surgery. Treatment usually combines infliximab with surgical management (examination under anaesthesia, seton placement to maintain drainage). Oral prednisolone does not heal fistulae and may worsen perianal sepsis. Methotrexate has some evidence in luminal Crohn's but less for fistulising disease. Lifelong ciprofloxacin is not recommended due to side effects (tendinopathy, neuropathy) and resistance. Vedolizumab (gut-selective anti-integrin) has emerging evidence for perianal disease but infliximab remains the first-line biologic choice.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 68-year-old man is found to have a 25 mm sessile polyp in the sigmoid colon during a routine bowel cancer screening colonoscopy. The polyp is removed endoscopically and histology shows a tubulovillous adenoma with high-grade dysplasia. The resection margin is clear. What is the most appropriate follow-up?
Option_A: Colonoscopy surveillance at 3 months then annually
Option_B: Colonoscopy surveillance at 1 year as per BSG post-polypectomy guidelines
Option_C: No follow-up needed as the margin is clear
Option_D: Referral for surgical sigmoid resection
Option_E: Repeat colonoscopy in 5 years as per routine screening
Explanation: BSG post-polypectomy surveillance guidelines recommend risk-stratified follow-up based on polyp characteristics. A polyp >=20 mm with high-grade dysplasia is classified as high-risk. For high-risk findings, colonoscopy surveillance at 1 year is recommended to check for recurrence at the polypectomy site and metachronous lesions. If the 1-year colonoscopy is clear, the interval can be extended to 3 years. No follow-up would be inappropriate given the high-risk features. Surveillance at 3 months is typically for piecemeal resections where completeness is uncertain, which is not specified here. Routine 5-year screening intervals apply to average-risk individuals without significant polyps. Surgical resection is not indicated when the polyp has been completely excised endoscopically with clear margins, even with high-grade dysplasia.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 50-year-old woman with a 15-year history of extensive ulcerative colitis (pancolitis) attends for review. She has been in remission on maintenance mesalazine for the past 3 years. According to BSG guidelines, what is the recommended colorectal cancer surveillance strategy?
Option_A: Colonoscopy every 1-2 years starting from diagnosis
Option_B: Colonoscopy every 5 years starting 10 years after diagnosis
Option_C: Colonoscopic surveillance starting 8-10 years after symptom onset with interval determined by risk factors
Option_D: No surveillance is needed while in remission
Option_E: Surveillance colonoscopy only if symptoms return
Explanation: BSG IBD guidelines recommend that patients with extensive UC (disease extending beyond the splenic flexure) should enter a colonoscopic surveillance programme starting 8-10 years after symptom onset. The surveillance interval is determined by individual risk factors: high-risk patients (extensive active inflammation, primary sclerosing cholangitis, stricture, previous dysplasia, family history of CRC <50) should have colonoscopy every 1 year; intermediate-risk patients every 2-3 years; and lower-risk patients every 5 years. Chronic colonic inflammation in UC significantly increases the risk of colorectal cancer over time. Surveillance is needed regardless of current remission status — cancer risk relates to cumulative inflammatory burden over years. Starting from diagnosis rather than symptom onset would be slightly premature. Annual colonoscopy from diagnosis is too frequent for most patients.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 78-year-old man presents with acute left iliac fossa pain and PR bleeding. He has a history of atrial fibrillation and is on apixaban. CT abdomen shows thickening of the sigmoid and descending colon wall with mucosal enhancement but no perforation. The distribution follows a watershed territory. Colonoscopy shows patchy oedematous mucosa with petechial haemorrhages and a single-stripe sign. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute diverticulitis
Option_B: Clostridium difficile colitis
Option_C: Colorectal carcinoma
Option_D: Ischaemic colitis
Option_E: Ulcerative colitis flare
Explanation: The clinical and investigative findings point to ischaemic colitis: elderly patient with vascular risk factors (AF requiring anticoagulation suggests cardiovascular disease), acute onset of left-sided abdominal pain with PR bleeding, CT showing colonic wall thickening in a watershed territory (the splenic flexure and sigmoid are most vulnerable due to their blood supply from the marginal artery), and colonoscopic findings of patchy oedematous mucosa with petechiae and the single-stripe sign (a linear ulcer along the antimesenteric border — pathognomonic for ischaemic colitis). Ischaemic colitis is caused by transient hypoperfusion of the colonic blood supply. Most cases are non-gangrenous and managed conservatively (IV fluids, bowel rest, monitoring). Diverticulitis typically shows pericolic fat-stranding centred on diverticula. UC causes continuous inflammation from the rectum. C. difficile causes diffuse colitis with pseudomembranes. Colorectal cancer presents as a mass lesion.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 45-year-old woman presents to her GP with fatigue and is found to have abnormal liver function tests. Her results show: bilirubin 18 umol/L (normal <21), ALT 185 IU/L (normal <40), AST 142 IU/L (normal <40), ALP 95 IU/L (normal 30-130), GGT 48 IU/L (normal <55), albumin 38 g/L (normal 35-50). What pattern of liver injury does this represent?
Option_A: Cholestatic pattern
Option_B: Hepatitic (hepatocellular) pattern
Option_C: Infiltrative pattern
Option_D: Mixed hepatitic and cholestatic pattern
Option_E: Normal liver function tests
Explanation: This LFT profile shows a hepatitic (hepatocellular) pattern: markedly raised transaminases (ALT and AST — both >3x upper limit of normal) with normal ALP and GGT. The transaminases are markers of hepatocyte damage/necrosis. The R factor (ALT/ULN of ALT divided by ALP/ULN of ALP) can help distinguish patterns: R >5 = hepatitic, R <2 = cholestatic, R 2-5 = mixed. Here the ALT is grossly elevated with normal ALP, giving a high R factor — clearly hepatitic. Common causes include viral hepatitis (A, B, C, E), drug-induced liver injury (e.g., paracetamol, statins), autoimmune hepatitis, alcohol, and Wilson's disease. A cholestatic pattern would show predominant ALP and GGT elevation with relatively normal transaminases. The bilirubin and albumin are normal, suggesting preserved synthetic function.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 22-year-old man presents to the emergency department 6 hours after ingesting 32 tablets of paracetamol 500 mg (total 16 g) in a single overdose. He is asymptomatic. His blood tests show: ALT 28 IU/L, INR 1.0, creatinine 72 umol/L, and paracetamol level 180 mg/L. The paracetamol level plotted on the treatment nomogram falls above the treatment line. What is the most appropriate management?
Option_A: Administer activated charcoal as this is within the treatment window
Option_B: Discharge with safety-netting advice as he is currently asymptomatic
Option_C: Observe for 24 hours and repeat paracetamol level before deciding on treatment
Option_D: Start intravenous N-acetylcysteine (NAC) immediately
Option_E: Start oral methionine as an alternative to NAC
Explanation: This patient has taken a significant paracetamol overdose (16 g — well above the 75 mg/kg threshold for potential toxicity) and his serum paracetamol level at 6 hours plots above the treatment line on the nomogram. According to the MHRA/NPIS paracetamol overdose guidelines, intravenous N-acetylcysteine (NAC) should be started immediately when the level is above the treatment line. NAC is the antidote for paracetamol poisoning — it replenishes glutathione stores, which are depleted during paracetamol metabolism, preventing the toxic metabolite NAPQI from causing hepatocellular necrosis. Being asymptomatic at 6 hours is expected — liver damage from paracetamol typically develops 24-72 hours post-ingestion, by which time it may be too late for NAC to be effective. Activated charcoal is only effective within 1 hour of ingestion. Observation without treatment when the level is above the line would risk fatal hepatotoxicity. Oral methionine is rarely used and is not first-line.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 55-year-old man with known alcohol-related liver cirrhosis presents with increasing abdominal distension over 2 weeks. On examination, there is shifting dullness and a fluid thrill. He is not on any diuretic therapy. A diagnostic ascitic tap is performed. The serum-ascites albumin gradient (SAAG) is 18 g/L. What does this SAAG value indicate?
Option_A: Ascites due to malignancy (peritoneal carcinomatosis)
Option_B: Ascites due to nephrotic syndrome
Option_C: Ascites due to tuberculosis
Option_D: Portal hypertension-related ascites
Option_E: The SAAG is inconclusive and further testing is needed
Explanation: The serum-ascites albumin gradient (SAAG) is calculated as serum albumin minus ascitic fluid albumin. A SAAG >=11 g/L indicates ascites caused by portal hypertension (sensitivity >97%). Common causes include liver cirrhosis (the most common), alcoholic hepatitis, cardiac failure, Budd-Chiari syndrome, and portal vein thrombosis. A SAAG <11 g/L indicates non-portal hypertensive causes such as peritoneal carcinomatosis, tuberculosis, pancreatitis, or nephrotic syndrome. This patient's SAAG of 18 g/L is clearly >=11, consistent with portal hypertension-related ascites, which fits his known cirrhosis. The SAAG has replaced the older exudate/transudate classification for ascites as it is more accurate for identifying the underlying mechanism.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 58-year-old man with alcohol-related liver cirrhosis and ascites is started on first-line diuretic therapy. He has no hepatic encephalopathy and his renal function is normal (creatinine 85 umol/L). According to BSG and EASL guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial diuretic regimen?
Option_A: Bumetanide 1 mg daily
Option_B: Furosemide 40 mg daily alone
Option_C: Spironolactone 100 mg daily alone or combined with furosemide 40 mg daily
Option_D: Spironolactone 400 mg daily
Option_E: Metolazone 5 mg daily
Explanation: BSG and EASL guidelines recommend spironolactone (an aldosterone antagonist) as the first-line diuretic for cirrhotic ascites, starting at 100 mg daily. It can be used alone or in combination with furosemide 40 mg daily from the outset. Spironolactone is preferred because secondary hyperaldosteronism is a key driver of sodium and water retention in cirrhosis. The doses can be titrated upward (maintaining a 100:40 ratio of spironolactone to furosemide) to a maximum of spironolactone 400 mg and furosemide 160 mg daily. Furosemide alone is less effective than spironolactone for cirrhotic ascites. Bumetanide and metolazone are not first-line. Starting at the maximum dose of spironolactone (400 mg) risks hyperkalaemia, renal impairment, and gynaecomastia. Weight loss and urinary sodium excretion should be monitored to assess response, aiming for weight loss of no more than 0.5 kg/day (or 1 kg/day if peripheral oedema is present).

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 62-year-old man with cirrhosis and ascites undergoes a diagnostic ascitic tap as part of an acute admission with abdominal pain and fever. The ascitic fluid results show: WCC 580 cells/mm³ with 320 neutrophils/mm³, protein 12 g/L, and ascitic culture pending. What is the diagnosis and immediate management?
Option_A: Malignant ascites — arrange CT abdomen and oncology referral
Option_B: Pancreatic ascites — check ascitic amylase
Option_C: Secondary bacterial peritonitis — arrange urgent CT to look for perforation
Option_D: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) — start IV cefotaxime immediately
Option_E: Uncomplicated ascites — start diuretics and discharge
Explanation: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) is diagnosed when the ascitic fluid neutrophil count is >=250 cells/mm³, regardless of culture results (cultures are negative in ~40% of SBP cases — culture-negative SBP). This patient has 320 neutrophils/mm³, confirming SBP. BSG and EASL guidelines recommend immediate empirical intravenous antibiotics — cefotaxime (or co-amoxiclav as an alternative) is the first-line treatment and should be started without waiting for culture results. SBP is a serious complication of cirrhotic ascites with significant mortality if untreated. IV albumin (1.5 g/kg on day 1, 1 g/kg on day 3) should also be given to reduce the risk of hepatorenal syndrome. Secondary peritonitis (from perforation) should be suspected if there are multiple organisms, very high neutrophils, or failure to respond to antibiotics, but the initial management is the same. This is not uncomplicated ascites given the raised neutrophil count.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 50-year-old woman presents with jaundice, pruritus, and fatigue. Her blood tests show: bilirubin 65 umol/L, ALT 58 IU/L, ALP 450 IU/L, GGT 380 IU/L, albumin 32 g/L. Anti-mitochondrial antibodies (AMA) are strongly positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Alcoholic hepatitis
Option_B: Autoimmune hepatitis
Option_C: Gilbert syndrome
Option_D: Primary biliary cholangitis
Option_E: Primary sclerosing cholangitis
Explanation: The LFT pattern shows a cholestatic picture (markedly raised ALP and GGT with mildly elevated ALT) combined with jaundice and pruritus. The strongly positive anti-mitochondrial antibodies (AMA) are highly specific (>95%) for primary biliary cholangitis (PBC, formerly primary biliary cirrhosis). PBC is a chronic autoimmune cholestatic liver disease characterised by progressive destruction of small intrahepatic bile ducts, leading to cholestasis, fibrosis, and eventually cirrhosis. It predominantly affects middle-aged women. First-line treatment is ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA), which improves biochemistry and delays disease progression. Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) also causes a cholestatic picture but is associated with p-ANCA antibodies and IBD (particularly UC), not AMA. Autoimmune hepatitis causes a hepatitic pattern with positive anti-smooth muscle or anti-LKM antibodies. Gilbert syndrome causes isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia with otherwise normal LFTs.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 48-year-old man with alcohol-related liver cirrhosis presents with confusion and agitation. His wife reports he has been increasingly drowsy over the past 2 days. On examination, he has a liver flap (asterixis), fetor hepaticus, and is disorientated in time and place. His ammonia level is elevated. A urine dipstick is positive for infection. What is the most likely precipitant and most appropriate initial management?
Option_A: Constipation — start lactulose 15-20 mL three times daily
Option_B: Dehydration — give 2 L normal saline over 4 hours
Option_C: GI bleeding — arrange urgent endoscopy
Option_D: Infection (UTI) — treat the infection and start oral lactulose
Option_E: Medication side effect — stop all hepatotoxic drugs
Explanation: This patient has hepatic encephalopathy (HE) — characterised by confusion, drowsiness, asterixis (liver flap), and fetor hepaticus in the context of cirrhosis. Elevated ammonia supports the diagnosis. The most common precipitants of HE are: infection (most common — UTI in this case), constipation, GI bleeding, dehydration/electrolyte imbalance, medications (sedatives, opioids), and excessive dietary protein. The urine dipstick is positive, indicating a UTI as the precipitant. Management of HE involves: (1) identifying and treating the precipitant — in this case, antibiotics for the UTI; (2) starting lactulose (15-20 mL titrated to 2-3 soft stools per day) to reduce ammonia absorption; (3) rifaximin may be added for secondary prophylaxis after a second episode. While constipation and dehydration can precipitate HE, the UTI is the identified precipitant here and must be treated alongside lactulose.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 35-year-old woman presents to the emergency department having taken an unknown number of paracetamol tablets at an unknown time (staggered overdose over 2 days). She is vomiting and has right upper quadrant tenderness. Her blood tests show: ALT 2500 IU/L, INR 3.8, creatinine 165 umol/L, pH 7.30, lactate 4.5 mmol/L, paracetamol level 45 mg/L. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
Option_A: Check a paracetamol level on the nomogram and treat accordingly
Option_B: Contact the regional liver transplant centre and start IV NAC immediately
Option_C: Start IV NAC and arrange outpatient hepatology follow-up in 1 week
Option_D: Start oral activated charcoal and observe
Option_E: Wait for repeat blood tests in 4 hours before starting treatment
Explanation: This is a staggered paracetamol overdose (taken over >1 hour) with features of established liver failure: massively elevated ALT (2500 IU/L — indicating severe hepatocyte necrosis), coagulopathy (INR 3.8), acute kidney injury (creatinine 165), metabolic acidosis (pH 7.30), and elevated lactate. The paracetamol nomogram CANNOT be used for staggered overdoses — it is only valid for single acute ingestions with a known time. For staggered overdoses, NPIS/MHRA guidelines recommend starting IV NAC immediately if the total dose exceeds 75 mg/kg over 24 hours, regardless of the paracetamol level. This patient already has evidence of severe hepatotoxicity meeting some of the King's College criteria for poor prognosis in paracetamol-induced liver failure (pH <7.3 is an independent criterion for transplant listing). The regional liver transplant centre must be contacted urgently. Activated charcoal is useless hours-days after ingestion. Any delay in treatment risks death.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 42-year-old man who drinks approximately 50 units of alcohol per week presents with jaundice, tender hepatomegaly, and fever. His blood tests show: bilirubin 120 umol/L, AST 210 IU/L, ALT 85 IU/L, ALP 150 IU/L, GGT 620 IU/L, albumin 28 g/L, INR 1.8, WCC 15 × 10⁹/L. His Maddrey discriminant function is calculated as 42. What does this score indicate and what is the most appropriate treatment?
Option_A: Maddrey's <32 — no specific treatment beyond supportive care
Option_B: Maddrey's >=32 — consider prednisolone 40 mg daily for 28 days if no contraindications
Option_C: Maddrey's >=32 — start azathioprine immediately
Option_D: Maddrey's >=32 — start N-acetylcysteine as definitive treatment
Option_E: Maddrey's score is not relevant in alcoholic hepatitis
Explanation: The Maddrey discriminant function (DF) is used to assess severity and guide treatment in alcoholic hepatitis. It is calculated as: 4.6 × (patient's PT − control PT) + bilirubin (umol/L ÷ 17). A DF >=32 indicates severe alcoholic hepatitis with significant short-term mortality (approximately 35-50% at 28 days without treatment). BSG and EASL guidelines recommend considering oral prednisolone 40 mg daily for 28 days (with a 7-day taper) for patients with a Maddrey DF >=32, provided there are no contraindications (active GI bleeding, uncontrolled sepsis, renal failure). The Lille score is assessed at day 7 to determine whether the patient is responding to steroids. Note the characteristic LFT pattern in alcoholic hepatitis: AST:ALT ratio >2:1, markedly raised GGT, raised bilirubin, and low albumin/raised INR (poor synthetic function). NAC may be used as adjunct therapy but is not definitive. Azathioprine has no role in alcoholic hepatitis.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 30-year-old woman presents with fatigue, arthralgia, and jaundice. She has no history of alcohol excess or drug use. Blood tests show: ALT 850 IU/L, AST 620 IU/L, ALP 110 IU/L, bilirubin 85 umol/L, IgG 28 g/L (normal 6-16), and positive anti-smooth muscle antibodies (SMA). Viral hepatitis screen is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Alcoholic hepatitis
Option_B: Autoimmune hepatitis
Option_C: Drug-induced liver injury
Option_D: Primary biliary cholangitis
Option_E: Wilson's disease
Explanation: The clinical features — a young woman with a hepatitic LFT pattern (markedly raised ALT and AST with relatively normal ALP), raised IgG, positive anti-smooth muscle antibodies (SMA), negative viral screen, and extrahepatic features (arthralgia) — are characteristic of autoimmune hepatitis (AIH). AIH is more common in women and can present at any age. Other associated autoantibodies include anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) and anti-LKM-1 (more common in children/young adults). Liver biopsy typically shows interface hepatitis with plasma cell infiltration. Treatment is with corticosteroids (prednisolone) for induction followed by azathioprine as a steroid-sparing maintenance agent. PBC would show a cholestatic pattern with AMA positivity. Alcoholic hepatitis has an AST:ALT ratio >2:1 and typically occurs in heavy drinkers. Wilson's disease should be considered (caeruloplasmin level) but positive SMA and raised IgG strongly favour AIH.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A GP discovers the following liver function test results in a 25-year-old man who presented for a routine health check: bilirubin 42 umol/L (normal <21), ALT 22 IU/L, ALP 78 IU/L, albumin 42 g/L. His FBC and reticulocyte count are normal. The bilirubin is predominantly unconjugated. He feels well and has no symptoms. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Crigler-Najjar syndrome
Option_B: Drug-induced hepatitis
Option_C: Gilbert syndrome
Option_D: Haemolytic anaemia
Option_E: Hepatitis B infection
Explanation: Gilbert syndrome is the most common cause of isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia in an otherwise well young person with normal liver function tests and no evidence of haemolysis (normal FBC and reticulocyte count). It affects approximately 5-10% of the population and is caused by reduced activity of UDP-glucuronosyltransferase (UGT1A1) enzyme responsible for bilirubin conjugation. Bilirubin levels typically fluctuate and may rise during fasting, illness, stress, or dehydration. Gilbert syndrome is benign and requires no treatment — simply reassurance. Haemolytic anaemia causes unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia but would show raised reticulocytes and anaemia. Crigler-Najjar syndrome is much rarer and presents in neonates/infancy with very high bilirubin levels. Hepatitis B and drug-induced hepatitis would cause raised transaminases. No further investigation is typically needed once the pattern is recognised.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 60-year-old man with known liver cirrhosis secondary to hepatitis C undergoes 6-monthly surveillance. An ultrasound shows a 2.5 cm hypoechoic lesion in the right lobe of the liver. His alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is elevated at 350 ng/mL (normal <10). What is the most likely diagnosis and next step?
Option_A: Benign liver haemangioma — repeat ultrasound in 6 months
Option_B: Focal nodular hyperplasia — no further action
Option_C: Hepatocellular carcinoma — arrange multiphase CT or MRI liver and discuss at hepatobiliary MDT
Option_D: Liver abscess — start IV antibiotics
Option_E: Metastatic colorectal cancer — arrange colonoscopy
Explanation: In a patient with liver cirrhosis, a new liver lesion with elevated AFP is highly suspicious for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). Cirrhosis (from any cause — hepatitis B/C, alcohol, NAFLD) is the major risk factor for HCC. NICE NG50 and EASL/AASLD guidelines recommend 6-monthly ultrasound surveillance ± AFP for patients with cirrhosis to detect HCC early. When a suspicious lesion is found, the next step is multiphase contrast-enhanced CT or MRI liver. HCC has a characteristic arterial enhancement pattern with washout in the portal venous/delayed phases. The case should be discussed at the hepatobiliary MDT for staging (Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer staging) and treatment planning (options include resection, transplantation, ablation, TACE, or sorafenib depending on stage). Haemangiomas and FNH would not cause elevated AFP. A liver abscess presents with fever and sepsis. Metastatic disease is less likely than primary HCC in a cirrhotic liver with raised AFP.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 52-year-old obese woman (BMI 38) with type 2 diabetes and hyperlipidaemia is found to have persistently elevated ALT (65 IU/L) on two occasions 3 months apart. She does not drink alcohol. Hepatitis B and C serology are negative. Autoimmune screen is negative. An ultrasound shows a bright echogenic liver consistent with hepatic steatosis. What is the most likely diagnosis and the most important management strategy?
Option_A: Alcoholic fatty liver disease — advise alcohol reduction
Option_B: Autoimmune hepatitis — start prednisolone
Option_C: Drug-induced liver injury — stop statins
Option_D: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD/MASLD) — weight loss through diet and exercise
Option_E: Primary biliary cholangitis — start ursodeoxycholic acid
Explanation: This patient has metabolic-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD, formerly NAFLD/NASH): she has metabolic risk factors (obesity, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidaemia), persistently elevated ALT, ultrasound evidence of steatosis, and other causes of liver disease have been excluded. NICE NG49 recommends that the most important management is weight loss through dietary modification and increased physical activity — a weight loss of 7-10% of body weight can improve steatohepatitis and even fibrosis. NAFLD/MASLD is now the most common cause of chronic liver disease in the UK. The enhanced liver fibrosis (ELF) test or FIB-4 score should be used to assess for fibrosis and identify patients who need hepatology referral. There are currently no licensed pharmacological therapies specifically for NAFLD, although pioglitazone and vitamin E have been studied. Statins should NOT be stopped — they are safe in NAFLD and indicated for her cardiovascular risk. Alcohol, autoimmune, and PBC have been excluded.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 48-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe epigastric pain radiating to her back, associated with nausea and vomiting. She drinks approximately 30 units of alcohol per week. Blood tests show: amylase 1,850 U/L (normal <100), WCC 14.2 × 10⁹/L, CRP 95 mg/L, glucose 12.5 mmol/L, LDH 450 U/L, AST 280 U/L, calcium 1.85 mmol/L, urea 18 mmol/L, PaO2 7.2 kPa. Using the modified Glasgow (Imrie) criteria, how many criteria does this patient meet and what does this indicate about severity?
Option_A: She meets 2 criteria — this is mild pancreatitis and she can be managed conservatively on a general ward
Option_B: She meets 3 or more criteria — this is predicted severe pancreatitis requiring HDU/ICU monitoring and aggressive fluid resuscitation
Option_C: She meets 1 criterion — this is mild pancreatitis requiring no further monitoring
Option_D: The Glasgow criteria cannot be applied within the first 48 hours
Option_E: The amylase alone confirms severe disease — scoring systems are not needed
Explanation: B
Difficulty: The modified Glasgow (Imrie) criteria predict severity in acute pancreatitis. The mnemonic PANCREAS helps recall: PaO2 <8 kPa (YES — 7.2), Age >55 (NO — 48), Neutrophils/WCC >15 (NO — 14.2), Calcium <2 mmol/L (YES — 1.85), Renal function urea >16 (YES — 18), Enzymes LDH >600 or AST >200 (YES — AST 280), Albumin <32 (not given — assume no), Sugar/glucose >10 (YES — 12.5). She meets at least 5 criteria. A score of ≥3 within 48 hours of onset predicts severe pancreatitis, which carries significant morbidity and mortality. This patient requires HDU/ICU-level care, aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, regular monitoring of organ function, and consideration of CT pancreas if she deteriorates. The Glasgow criteria are specifically designed for use within the first 48 hours. Amylase level does not correlate with severity — a very high amylase does not necessarily mean severe disease.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 55-year-old woman presents with right upper quadrant pain, fever (38.8°C), and jaundice. She has been vomiting and appears systemically unwell with a heart rate of 110 bpm and blood pressure of 95/60 mmHg. Blood tests show: bilirubin 85 μmol/L, ALP 420 U/L, WCC 18.5 × 10⁹/L, CRP 210 mg/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute cholangitis
Option_B: Acute cholecystitis
Option_C: Biliary colic
Option_D: Hepatitis A infection
Option_E: Pancreatic head carcinoma
Explanation: A
Difficulty: This patient presents with Charcot triad: right upper quadrant pain, fever, and jaundice — the classic presentation of acute cholangitis (infection of the biliary tree, usually caused by common bile duct obstruction from gallstones). The presence of tachycardia and hypotension adds haemodynamic compromise, forming Reynolds pentad (Charcot triad + confusion + shock), which indicates suppurative cholangitis — a life-threatening emergency requiring urgent resuscitation, IV antibiotics, and emergency biliary decompression (usually via ERCP). The cholestatic blood picture (raised bilirubin and ALP) with raised inflammatory markers supports biliary obstruction with infection. Acute cholecystitis causes RUQ pain and fever but typically does not cause jaundice unless there is concurrent bile duct obstruction. Biliary colic is intermittent RUQ pain without fever or jaundice. Hepatitis causes jaundice but with a hepatitic (transaminase-predominant) pattern and typically without the acute surgical presentation. Pancreatic head carcinoma causes painless obstructive jaundice.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 42-year-old woman presents with intermittent right upper quadrant pain after eating fatty meals. The pain typically lasts 1-3 hours and then resolves completely. She is afebrile and non-jaundiced. Abdominal examination is unremarkable between episodes. An abdominal ultrasound shows multiple gallstones in the gallbladder with no bile duct dilatation. Liver function tests are normal. What is the most appropriate management?
Option_A: Emergency cholecystectomy within 24 hours
Option_B: ERCP to clear the bile duct
Option_C: Elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy
Option_D: Ursodeoxycholic acid to dissolve the gallstones
Option_E: Watchful waiting — no treatment is needed unless complications develop
Explanation: C
Difficulty: This patient has symptomatic gallstone disease presenting as biliary colic — episodic RUQ pain triggered by fatty food, lasting <6 hours, with complete resolution between episodes. NICE NG104 recommends elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy for patients with symptomatic gallstones. Surgery is offered because once gallstones become symptomatic, there is a significant risk of complications (acute cholecystitis, pancreatitis, cholangitis). Emergency cholecystectomy is reserved for acute cholecystitis (within 1 week of presentation per NICE guidelines). ERCP is indicated for bile duct stones but this patient has normal LFTs and no duct dilatation, so duct stones are unlikely. Ursodeoxycholic acid has very limited efficacy — it only works on small cholesterol stones, takes months, and stones frequently recur after stopping. It is not recommended as standard treatment. Watchful waiting would be appropriate for incidentally found asymptomatic gallstones, but this patient is symptomatic.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 62-year-old man presents with painless progressive jaundice over 3 weeks. He has noticed dark urine, pale stools, and generalised pruritus. He has lost 8 kg in weight over 2 months. On examination, a smooth non-tender mass is palpable in the right upper quadrant below the liver edge. Blood tests show: bilirubin 180 μmol/L, ALP 650 U/L, GGT 380 U/L, ALT 55 U/L. What does the palpable mass most likely represent and what is the most likely underlying diagnosis?
Option_A: Enlarged gallbladder due to gallstone obstruction of the cystic duct (mucocoele)
Option_B: Hepatomegaly from metastatic liver disease
Option_C: Palpable gallbladder secondary to pancreatic head carcinoma obstructing the common bile duct (Courvoisier sign)
Option_D: Right kidney mass causing extrinsic bile duct compression
Option_E: Splenic enlargement from portal hypertension
Explanation: C
Difficulty: This is a classic presentation of Courvoisier sign (law): painless obstructive jaundice with a palpable gallbladder suggests the obstruction is NOT caused by gallstones (which cause chronic inflammation and fibrosis of the gallbladder wall, preventing distension) but rather by an extrinsic malignant compression — most commonly pancreatic head carcinoma. The clinical picture is textbook: painless progressive jaundice, weight loss, dark urine and pale stools (obstructive pattern), pruritus (from bile salt deposition), and a cholestatic LFT pattern (markedly raised ALP and GGT with disproportionately raised bilirubin and only mildly elevated ALT). Investigation should proceed with CT pancreas (pancreatic protocol) as the initial imaging modality. A gallstone mucocoele causes a palpable gallbladder but does not typically cause obstructive jaundice. Hepatomegaly would be felt as an enlarged liver edge, not a discrete RUQ mass. Portal hypertension causes splenomegaly (left-sided).

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 38-year-old man with a history of heavy alcohol use presents with a 6-month history of recurrent epigastric pain, steatorrhoea, and weight loss. A CT abdomen shows pancreatic calcifications and ductal dilatation. His HbA1c is 58 mmol/mol. What is the most appropriate treatment for his steatorrhoea?
Option_A: Bile acid sequestrants (cholestyramine)
Option_B: Loperamide to reduce stool frequency
Option_C: Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) with meals
Option_D: Proton pump inhibitor monotherapy
Option_E: Strict low-fat diet alone without enzyme supplementation
Explanation: C
Difficulty: This patient has chronic pancreatitis (alcohol-related) with pancreatic exocrine insufficiency causing steatorrhoea (fatty, foul-smelling stools due to fat malabsorption) and endocrine insufficiency (secondary diabetes — HbA1c 58). The pancreatic calcifications and ductal dilatation on CT are characteristic. NICE NG104 recommends pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT — e.g., Creon) for patients with pancreatic exocrine insufficiency. PERT capsules contain lipase, amylase, and protease and must be taken with meals and snacks to aid fat digestion. The dose is titrated according to symptoms and stool fat content. A PPI may be added if PERT alone is insufficient, as gastric acid can inactivate the enzymes — but PPI alone does not treat steatorrhoea. Loperamide reduces stool frequency but does not address the underlying malabsorption. Bile acid sequestrants treat bile acid malabsorption, not pancreatic exocrine insufficiency. A low-fat diet alone is insufficient — patients need PERT to absorb fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 45-year-old woman is admitted with acute pancreatitis secondary to gallstones. She is managed with IV fluids, analgesia, and nil by mouth initially. Her symptoms improve over 48 hours and she begins eating. According to NICE NG104, when should cholecystectomy be performed to prevent recurrence?
Option_A: Cholecystectomy should be performed during the same admission or within 2 weeks
Option_B: Cholecystectomy should be performed at 3 months to allow inflammation to settle
Option_C: Cholecystectomy should be performed at 6 months
Option_D: Cholecystectomy is not indicated after a single episode of gallstone pancreatitis
Option_E: ERCP with sphincterotomy alone is sufficient — cholecystectomy is not needed
Explanation: A
Difficulty: NICE NG104 recommends that patients with gallstone pancreatitis should have cholecystectomy during the same admission or within 2 weeks of the acute episode. This is because the risk of recurrent gallstone pancreatitis is highest in the early weeks after the index episode (up to 25-30% within 6 weeks if untreated). Delaying surgery to 3 or 6 months exposes the patient to an unacceptable risk of recurrence, which may be more severe. If the patient is too unwell for surgery during the index admission, it should be performed as soon as possible after recovery. ERCP is indicated if there are bile duct stones (suggested by a dilated CBD or deranged LFTs) but does not replace cholecystectomy — the gallbladder remains the source of stones. This is one of the most important updates in recent NICE guidance and a commonly tested point.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 70-year-old man presents to his GP with a 4-week history of progressive painless jaundice, 6 kg weight loss, and new-onset back pain. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history and type 2 diabetes diagnosed 6 months ago. Blood tests show an obstructive LFT pattern. A CT abdomen with pancreatic protocol shows a 3.5 cm mass in the body of the pancreas with encasement of the superior mesenteric artery. What is the most appropriate next step?
Option_A: Attempted surgical resection (Whipple procedure) as this offers the only chance of cure
Option_B: Endoscopic ultrasound-guided biopsy for tissue diagnosis and MDT discussion
Option_C: Immediate palliative care referral without further investigation
Option_D: Start empirical chemotherapy based on the CT findings
Option_E: Trial of pancreatic enzyme replacement to see if symptoms improve
Explanation: B
Difficulty: The CT findings of a pancreatic body mass encasing the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) indicate locally advanced, borderline unresectable or unresectable pancreatic cancer. Arterial encasement (SMA, coeliac axis, or hepatic artery) is a criterion for borderline or locally advanced disease that precludes upfront surgical resection. The next step is tissue diagnosis via endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)-guided fine needle aspiration/biopsy, followed by multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion to determine the best management plan (neoadjuvant chemotherapy, palliative chemotherapy, or best supportive care). A Whipple procedure (pancreaticoduodenectomy) is only possible for resectable tumours without major vascular involvement — and is typically for pancreatic head lesions. Starting chemotherapy without histological confirmation is inappropriate. Immediate palliative care without tissue diagnosis would miss the opportunity for oncological treatment that may extend survival. PERT addresses exocrine insufficiency but does not treat the cancer.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 50-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with right upper quadrant pain, nausea, and vomiting for 18 hours. She has a temperature of 38.2°C and is tender in the right upper quadrant with a positive Murphy sign. Blood tests show WCC 15.8 × 10⁹/L and CRP 120 mg/L. Bilirubin and liver enzymes are normal. An abdominal ultrasound shows a thick-walled gallbladder with pericholecystic fluid and gallstones, with a normal common bile duct diameter. What is the most appropriate management according to NICE NG104?
Option_A: Discharge with oral antibiotics and outpatient surgical review in 6 weeks
Option_B: ERCP within 24 hours to exclude bile duct stones
Option_C: IV antibiotics and early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 1 week of presentation
Option_D: IV antibiotics alone and delayed cholecystectomy at 6-8 weeks
Option_E: Percutaneous cholecystostomy as first-line treatment
Explanation: C
Difficulty: This patient has acute cholecystitis: RUQ pain, fever, positive Murphy sign (inspiratory arrest on palpation of the RUQ), raised inflammatory markers, and ultrasound findings of a thick-walled gallbladder with pericholecystic fluid and stones. NICE NG104 recommends early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (within 1 week of presentation, ideally within 72 hours) for acute cholecystitis, alongside IV antibiotics. Early surgery has been shown to be safe and reduces total hospital stay, complications, and the risk of recurrent episodes compared with delayed surgery. The traditional approach of 'cool down' with antibiotics and delayed cholecystectomy at 6-8 weeks is no longer recommended by NICE due to the high rate of readmission with recurrent symptoms during the waiting period. ERCP is not indicated as the LFTs are normal and the CBD is not dilated (no evidence of duct stones). Percutaneous cholecystostomy is reserved for patients who are too unwell for surgery. Discharge on oral antibiotics alone is inappropriate for acute cholecystitis.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 72-year-old man is admitted to hospital following a fall. He weighs 52 kg and is 1.75 m tall (BMI 17 kg/m²). He has eaten very little for the past 10 days due to poor appetite. The nursing staff initiate nasogastric feeding at full target rate. On day 2, he develops confusion, peripheral oedema, and an irregular heart rhythm. Blood tests show: phosphate 0.35 mmol/L (normal 0.8-1.5), potassium 2.8 mmol/L, magnesium 0.4 mmol/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute heart failure secondary to fluid overload
Option_B: Hospital-acquired pneumonia with sepsis
Option_C: Refeeding syndrome
Option_D: Stroke causing acute confusion
Option_E: Vitamin B12 deficiency neuropathy
Explanation: C
Difficulty: This is a classic presentation of refeeding syndrome. The patient has multiple risk factors: BMI <18.5, prolonged poor oral intake (>10 days), and is elderly. Refeeding syndrome occurs when malnourished patients are fed too aggressively after a period of starvation. During starvation, the body shifts to fat and protein metabolism. When carbohydrate feeding resumes, insulin secretion surges, driving glucose and electrolytes (phosphate, potassium, magnesium) into cells. This causes profound hypophosphataemia (the hallmark biochemical feature), hypokalaemia, and hypomagnesaemia. Complications include cardiac arrhythmias, heart failure, respiratory failure, seizures, rhabdomyolysis, and death. NICE CG32 identifies high-risk patients and recommends: (1) starting feeding at no more than 10 kcal/kg/day (or 5 kcal/kg/day if BMI <14 or negligible intake >15 days), (2) supplementing thiamine, vitamin B complex, and a balanced multivitamin/trace element before feeding, (3) replacing phosphate, potassium, and magnesium before and during feeding, and (4) slowly increasing feeding over 4-7 days. This patient was fed at full rate without electrolyte monitoring or supplementation — a dangerous error.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 58-year-old woman is admitted to the surgical ward for an elective hip replacement. As part of the admission process, the nurse calculates her Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool (MUST) score. She weighs 60 kg and is 1.62 m tall (BMI 22.9 kg/m²). She has had no unplanned weight loss in the past 3-6 months and is expected to eat normally after surgery. What is her MUST score and what action is required?
Option_A: MUST score 0 (low risk) — routine clinical care and repeat screening weekly in hospital
Option_B: MUST score 1 (medium risk) — observe dietary intake for 3 days
Option_C: MUST score 2 (high risk) — refer to dietitian urgently
Option_D: MUST score 3 — start parenteral nutrition
Option_E: The MUST tool is not applicable to surgical patients
Explanation: A
Difficulty: The MUST (Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool) is a five-step screening tool recommended by NICE and BAPEN for all hospital admissions and community care contacts. It uses three criteria: (1) BMI score — BMI >20 scores 0, 18.5-20 scores 1, <18.5 scores 2; (2) Unplanned weight loss in past 3-6 months — <5% scores 0, 5-10% scores 1, >10% scores 2; (3) Acute disease effect — if the patient is acutely ill AND has had or is likely to have no nutritional intake for >5 days, add 2. This patient scores: BMI 22.9 = 0, no weight loss = 0, expected to eat normally = 0. Total MUST score = 0 (low risk). Action: routine clinical care with repeat screening weekly (inpatients) or monthly (community) or at each outpatient appointment. A score of 1 = medium risk (observe), ≥2 = high risk (treat — refer to dietitian, set dietary goals, consider oral nutritional supplements).

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 35-year-old woman presents to her GP with fatigue, recurrent mouth ulcers, and a smooth sore tongue. She follows a strict vegan diet and takes no supplements. Blood tests show: Hb 95 g/L (normal 120-150), MCV 108 fL (normal 80-100), WCC and platelets normal, serum B12 45 ng/L (normal 200-900), folate 8.2 μg/L (normal >3), ferritin 85 μg/L. What is the most likely cause of her anaemia?
Option_A: Coeliac disease causing malabsorption
Option_B: Folate deficiency from inadequate dietary intake
Option_C: Iron deficiency masked by a normal ferritin
Option_D: Pernicious anaemia with anti-intrinsic factor antibodies
Option_E: Vitamin B12 deficiency due to inadequate dietary intake
Explanation: E
Difficulty: This patient has a macrocytic anaemia (MCV 108) with a very low serum B12 (45 ng/L) and normal folate and ferritin. She follows a strict vegan diet with no supplements. Vitamin B12 is found almost exclusively in animal-derived foods (meat, fish, eggs, dairy). Vegans who do not supplement are at high risk of B12 deficiency, which causes megaloblastic anaemia, glossitis (smooth sore tongue), and mouth ulcers. Neurological complications (subacute combined degeneration of the cord — peripheral neuropathy, ataxia, dorsal column signs) can develop if untreated. Treatment is with oral cyanocobalamin (high-dose 1-2 mg daily) or intramuscular hydroxocobalamin injections. Pernicious anaemia (autoimmune destruction of parietal cells/intrinsic factor) is an important differential but would be unusual at 35 in someone with a clear dietary explanation. Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies should be checked if the dietary history does not explain the deficiency or if she fails to respond to oral replacement. Folate is normal. Ferritin is normal, excluding iron deficiency.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 45-year-old man is admitted to the ICU following emergency surgery for a perforated duodenal ulcer. Post-operatively, he develops a prolonged ileus and is unable to tolerate oral or nasogastric feeding for 10 days. He is losing weight and becoming increasingly malnourished. What is the most appropriate nutritional support?
Option_A: Continue IV fluids with dextrose and wait for gut function to return
Option_B: Nasojejunal tube feeding to bypass the stomach
Option_C: Peripheral intravenous feeding via a short cannula
Option_D: Total parenteral nutrition (TPN) via a central venous catheter
Option_E: There is no indication for nutritional support — most patients tolerate short periods without feeding
Explanation: D
Difficulty: When the gastrointestinal tract is non-functional (prolonged ileus, short bowel, high-output fistula, complete bowel obstruction) and enteral feeding is not possible for an extended period, total parenteral nutrition (TPN) is indicated. TPN provides complete nutrition (carbohydrates, amino acids, lipids, electrolytes, vitamins, and trace elements) delivered via a dedicated lumen of a central venous catheter (e.g., PICC line or tunnelled central line). NICE CG32 recommends that nutritional support should be considered for anyone who has eaten little or nothing for more than 5 days, or is expected to eat little or nothing for the next 5 or more days. This patient has been unable to eat for 10 days and is losing weight. IV dextrose alone provides inadequate calories and no protein. Nasojejunal feeding would still require some gut function. Peripheral parenteral nutrition can be used short-term but is limited by osmolality and vein tolerance. TPN requires careful monitoring of blood glucose, electrolytes, and liver function, and carries risks including line infection, metabolic complications, and refeeding syndrome.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 78-year-old woman in a care home has a BMI of 16 kg/m² and has been eating poorly for 3 weeks. She is identified as high risk on MUST screening. Which of the following electrolytes should be checked and supplemented BEFORE refeeding is commenced?
Option_A: Calcium and vitamin D only
Option_B: Chloride and bicarbonate
Option_C: Phosphate potassium and magnesium — and thiamine should be given prophylactically
Option_D: Serum iron and transferrin saturation
Option_E: Sodium and glucose only
Explanation: C
Difficulty: Before commencing refeeding in a malnourished patient, NICE CG32 recommends checking and correcting phosphate, potassium, and magnesium levels, and giving prophylactic thiamine (vitamin B1) supplementation. During refeeding, the insulin surge drives phosphate, potassium, and magnesium intracellularly, causing potentially fatal drops in serum levels. Thiamine is an essential cofactor in carbohydrate metabolism — as glucose intake increases, thiamine demand rises sharply. Deficiency can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia). Thiamine should be given BEFORE the first feed and continued for at least 10 days. NICE also recommends a balanced multivitamin/trace element supplement and starting feeding slowly (10 kcal/kg/day for high-risk patients). Calcium and vitamin D are important but are not the critical electrolytes in refeeding syndrome. Sodium, chloride, and bicarbonate disturbances are not the hallmark of refeeding. Iron replacement is relevant for iron deficiency but not for refeeding prevention.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 55-year-old woman with a BMI of 42 kg/m² is reviewed by her GP. She has tried lifestyle modifications, orlistat, and a specialist weight management programme but her BMI remains above 40 kg/m² and she has developed type 2 diabetes, obstructive sleep apnoea, and knee osteoarthritis. According to NICE CG189, what is the most appropriate next step in her management?
Option_A: Continue orlistat at double the standard dose
Option_B: Prescribe very low-calorie diet (VLCD) indefinitely
Option_C: Refer for bariatric surgery assessment
Option_D: Start metformin for weight loss even without diabetes
Option_E: There are no further options — continue lifestyle modifications
Explanation: C
Difficulty: NICE CG189 recommends considering referral for bariatric surgery assessment for adults with: (1) BMI ≥40 kg/m², or (2) BMI 35-40 kg/m² with a significant obesity-related comorbidity that could be improved by weight loss (e.g., type 2 diabetes, OSA, cardiovascular disease), provided they have tried all appropriate non-surgical measures without achieving clinically beneficial weight loss. This patient meets criteria on multiple counts. Bariatric surgery (e.g., gastric bypass, sleeve gastrectomy) is the most effective intervention for sustained weight loss and can induce remission of type 2 diabetes. NICE also suggests expedited assessment for patients with BMI ≥35 and recent-onset T2DM (within 10 years). Orlistat has a maximum dose of 120 mg TDS and doubling is not standard practice. VLCDs are used as short-term interventions, not indefinitely. Metformin is for diabetes management, not a licensed weight-loss medication.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 30-year-old woman presents to her GP with fatigue, weight loss, and iron deficiency anaemia that has not responded to oral iron supplementation. She also reports intermittent bloating and loose stools. Tissue transglutaminase (tTG) antibodies are strongly positive. What is the next step to confirm the diagnosis?
Option_A: Commence a gluten-free diet as a therapeutic trial
Option_B: Duodenal biopsy via upper GI endoscopy while still on a gluten-containing diet
Option_C: HLA-DQ2/DQ8 genetic testing to confirm coeliac disease
Option_D: Repeat tTG antibodies in 3 months to confirm they remain elevated
Option_E: Stool calprotectin to exclude inflammatory bowel disease
Explanation: B
Difficulty: A strongly positive tTG IgA antibody result is highly suggestive of coeliac disease, but in adults, NICE NG20 recommends confirmatory duodenal biopsy via upper GI endoscopy. Crucially, the patient must remain on a gluten-containing diet at the time of biopsy — if gluten has been removed, the histological changes (villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, intraepithelial lymphocytosis — Marsh classification) may have resolved, leading to a false-negative result. NICE recommends eating gluten in more than one meal per day for at least 6 weeks before investigation. Commencing a gluten-free diet before biopsy would be premature and could compromise the diagnostic pathway. HLA-DQ2/DQ8 testing has a high negative predictive value (useful for excluding coeliac disease) but a positive result does not confirm it (30% of the general population carry these alleles). Repeating serology delays diagnosis. Stool calprotectin is used for IBD, not coeliac disease. Note: in children with tTG >10× ULN and positive EMA, a no-biopsy pathway exists — but this does not apply to adults.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 68-year-old woman presents to her GP with constipation. She opens her bowels twice per week with hard stools (Bristol stool type 1-2) and straining. She has no red flag symptoms (no rectal bleeding, weight loss, or change in bowel habit suggesting malignancy). She drinks limited fluids and has a low-fibre diet. She takes amlodipine and codeine for chronic pain. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
Option_A: Bisacodyl suppositories daily as first-line treatment
Option_B: Increase dietary fibre and fluid intake — review codeine as a contributing factor — and if lifestyle measures fail start osmotic laxative (macrogol)
Option_C: Lactulose 30 mL three times daily
Option_D: Prucalopride as first-line treatment
Option_E: Urgent colonoscopy to exclude colorectal cancer
Explanation: B
Difficulty: NICE CG99 (adapted from CKS guidance on constipation) recommends a stepwise approach. First-line: address lifestyle factors — increase dietary fibre (aim for 30 g/day from wholegrain foods, fruit, vegetables), increase fluid intake, and increase physical activity. Importantly, medications should be reviewed — codeine (an opioid) is a very common cause of drug-induced constipation and should be replaced if possible. If lifestyle measures are insufficient, NICE recommends an osmotic laxative (macrogol/polyethylene glycol, e.g., Movicol) as first-line pharmacological treatment, as it has a good evidence base and is well tolerated. Lactulose is an alternative osmotic laxative but can cause bloating and is generally considered less effective than macrogol. Bisacodyl (a stimulant laxative) can be added if osmotic laxatives alone are insufficient but is not first-line. Prucalopride is a prokinetic reserved for chronic constipation that has failed at least two different laxatives. Urgent colonoscopy is not indicated without red flags.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 50-year-old woman with known osteoporosis is found to have a vitamin D level of 18 nmol/L (deficient <25 nmol/L). She takes no regular medications. She is not acutely unwell. According to NICE and the Royal Osteoporosis Society, what is the most appropriate vitamin D replacement regimen?
Option_A: Calcitriol (active vitamin D) 0.25 micrograms daily
Option_B: High-dose loading with colecalciferol followed by a maintenance dose of 800-2000 IU daily
Option_C: Intramuscular vitamin D injection as a single dose — no follow-up needed
Option_D: Sunlight exposure alone is sufficient to correct deficiency
Option_E: Vitamin D 400 IU daily as standard dose
Explanation: B
Difficulty: For confirmed vitamin D deficiency (<25 nmol/L), treatment involves a loading phase followed by maintenance. The CKS/Royal Osteoporosis Society recommend a loading regimen of approximately 300,000 IU total colecalciferol over 6-10 weeks (e.g., 50,000 IU weekly for 6 weeks, or 4,000 IU daily for 10 weeks), followed by a maintenance dose of 800-2,000 IU daily long-term. This patient also has osteoporosis, so adequate vitamin D and calcium intake are essential alongside bone-specific treatment (e.g., bisphosphonate). Calcitriol (1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D) is the active form and is reserved for specific conditions (e.g., renal failure, hypoparathyroidism) — it is NOT used for routine vitamin D replacement as it bypasses the body's regulatory feedback mechanism and carries a higher risk of hypercalcaemia. 400 IU daily is a preventive dose for those at risk but is insufficient to treat established deficiency. Sunlight alone is unreliable in the UK. A single IM injection may be used in malabsorption but requires follow-up to confirm adequacy.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 65-year-old man presents to the emergency department with haematemesis. He has vomited approximately 500 mL of fresh blood and is feeling faint. He takes aspirin and naproxen for osteoarthritis. Observations: heart rate 115 bpm, blood pressure 88/52 mmHg, SpO2 97%. Blood tests show: Hb 78 g/L, urea 18.5 mmol/L, creatinine 95 μmol/L. What is the most important initial management priority?
Option_A: Arrange emergency upper GI endoscopy immediately before any other intervention
Option_B: Give IV proton pump inhibitor (omeprazole 80 mg bolus) as the first action
Option_C: Insert two large-bore IV cannulae and commence aggressive fluid resuscitation — activate the major haemorrhage protocol
Option_D: Prescribe oral tranexamic acid and observe for 4 hours
Option_E: Request an urgent CT angiogram to locate the bleeding point
Explanation: C
Difficulty: This patient is in haemorrhagic shock from an acute upper GI haemorrhage (haematemesis with tachycardia and hypotension). The immediate priority is ABC resuscitation: secure the airway, insert two large-bore (14G or 16G) IV cannulae, commence rapid crystalloid infusion, and activate the major haemorrhage protocol for urgent cross-matched blood (or O-negative if immediately life-threatening). NICE NG141 emphasises that resuscitation takes priority before any investigation. Blood should be sent for FBC, U&E, LFTs, coagulation, cross-match, and group and save. A Glasgow-Blatchford score should be calculated to risk-stratify: this patient scores highly (Hb <100, systolic BP <90, tachycardia, raised urea, melaena likely). Endoscopy should be performed within 24 hours (or within 2 hours if massive uncontrolled haemorrhage) but NOT before the patient is haemodynamically stabilised. IV PPI is given but is not the first action — resuscitation comes first. Tranexamic acid is not routinely recommended for upper GI bleeding (the HALT-IT trial showed no benefit and increased VTE risk).

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 58-year-old man with known alcohol-related liver cirrhosis presents with massive haematemesis. He is haemodynamically unstable (BP 80/45 mmHg, HR 125 bpm). After initial resuscitation, upper GI endoscopy reveals actively bleeding oesophageal varices. Band ligation is performed but bleeding continues. A Sengstaken-Blakemore tube is inserted as a temporising measure. Which intravenous drug should have been started at presentation and continued alongside endoscopic therapy?
Option_A: IV hydrocortisone to prevent adrenal crisis
Option_B: IV omeprazole infusion at 8 mg/hour
Option_C: IV terlipressin
Option_D: IV tranexamic acid
Option_E: Oral propranolol via nasogastric tube
Explanation: C
Difficulty: In acute variceal haemorrhage, NICE NG141 and BSG guidelines recommend starting IV terlipressin at presentation (before endoscopy) and continuing for up to 5 days. Terlipressin is a vasopressin analogue that causes splanchnic vasoconstriction, reducing portal pressure and variceal blood flow. It has been shown to reduce mortality in variceal bleeding. Alongside terlipressin, prophylactic IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone) should be given — infection is common in cirrhotic patients with variceal bleeding and antibiotics reduce rebleeding and mortality. Endoscopic band ligation is the definitive endoscopic treatment for oesophageal varices. If endoscopic therapy fails (as here), a Sengstaken-Blakemore tube provides temporary tamponade as a bridge to TIPSS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt). IV omeprazole is used for non-variceal upper GI bleeding (e.g., peptic ulcer) but is not indicated for variceal haemorrhage. Propranolol is for secondary prophylaxis of varices, not acute management. Tranexamic acid is not recommended in GI bleeding.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 72-year-old woman presents with acute onset colicky central abdominal pain, vomiting, abdominal distension, and absolute constipation (no flatus or stool for 24 hours). She had an open appendicectomy 30 years ago. Abdominal X-ray shows multiple dilated small bowel loops (>3 cm) with valvulae conniventes visible and no gas in the large bowel. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute mesenteric ischaemia
Option_B: Large bowel obstruction from a sigmoid volvulus
Option_C: Paralytic ileus secondary to medication
Option_D: Small bowel obstruction secondary to adhesions
Option_E: Toxic megacolon from inflammatory bowel disease
Explanation: D
Difficulty: This is a classic presentation of small bowel obstruction (SBO): colicky central abdominal pain, vomiting (often early and prominent in SBO), distension, and absolute constipation. The AXR shows dilated small bowel loops >3 cm with valvulae conniventes (the transverse folds that cross the full width of the small bowel — distinguishing it from large bowel haustra which do not cross the full width). The absence of large bowel gas suggests complete obstruction. The most common cause of SBO in developed countries is adhesions from previous abdominal surgery — this patient had an appendicectomy. Other causes include hernias, malignancy, and Crohn disease. Initial management is 'drip and suck': NBM, IV fluids, nasogastric tube decompression, and catheterisation to monitor urine output. Urgent surgical referral is needed. Signs of strangulation (continuous rather than colicky pain, peritonism, fever, tachycardia, raised lactate) mandate emergency surgery. Large bowel obstruction typically presents with later-onset vomiting and shows colonic dilatation. Paralytic ileus causes generalised dilatation without the colicky pain.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: An 82-year-old man presents to the emergency department with severe generalised abdominal pain of sudden onset 3 hours ago. He has atrial fibrillation and recently stopped his apixaban due to a dental procedure. On examination, the pain seems disproportionate to the clinical findings — the abdomen is soft with minimal tenderness. Blood tests show: WCC 16.5 × 10⁹/L, lactate 6.8 mmol/L, amylase mildly elevated at 180 U/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute mesenteric ischaemia
Option_B: Acute pancreatitis
Option_C: Perforated duodenal ulcer
Option_D: Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm
Option_E: Sigmoid volvulus
Explanation: A
Difficulty: The hallmark of acute mesenteric ischaemia is 'pain out of proportion to examination findings' — severe abdominal pain with a relatively benign-appearing abdomen (initially soft, non-peritonitic). This patient has major risk factors: atrial fibrillation (source of arterial embolism) and recent cessation of anticoagulation. The superior mesenteric artery is the most commonly affected vessel. The raised lactate (6.8 mmol/L) indicates bowel ischaemia and anaerobic metabolism — this is a critical finding. A mildly raised amylase can occur in mesenteric ischaemia and should not distract towards a diagnosis of pancreatitis (where amylase is typically >3× upper limit of normal). As bowel necrosis progresses, peritonism develops and the patient deteriorates rapidly with sepsis and multiorgan failure. This is a surgical emergency with very high mortality. CT angiography is the investigation of choice and should be performed urgently. Treatment involves emergency laparotomy with resection of non-viable bowel and potentially arterial revascularisation. A perforated ulcer would cause peritonism from the outset. AAA typically causes back/flank pain with haemodynamic instability.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 28-year-old woman with known ulcerative colitis (pan-colitis) is admitted with a severe flare: bloody diarrhoea >8 times/day, temperature 38.5°C, heart rate 105 bpm, Hb 98 g/L, CRP 85 mg/L, and albumin 28 g/L. On day 3 of IV hydrocortisone 100 mg QDS, she develops worsening abdominal distension and pain. An abdominal X-ray shows transverse colon diameter of 7.5 cm with mucosal islands. What is this complication and what is the immediate management?
Option_A: Acute mesenteric ischaemia — arrange CT angiography
Option_B: Clostridium difficile colitis — send stool sample and start oral vancomycin
Option_C: Paralytic ileus — insert nasogastric tube and continue steroids
Option_D: Toxic megacolon — urgent surgical review for potential subtotal colectomy
Option_E: Viral gastroenteritis superimposed on UC — supportive management only
Explanation: D
Difficulty: This patient has developed toxic megacolon — a life-threatening complication of severe ulcerative colitis (and occasionally Crohn colitis or C. difficile). The diagnostic criteria are: (1) radiographic evidence of colonic dilatation (transverse colon >5.5 cm — this patient has 7.5 cm) with (2) systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, raised inflammatory markers). Mucosal islands on AXR indicate severe mucosal inflammation and ulceration. Management requires urgent surgical review — the patient needs joint medical-surgical care. If there is no improvement within 24-72 hours of medical optimisation (IV steroids, IV fluids, VTE prophylaxis, NBM/limited oral intake), emergency subtotal colectomy with end ileostomy is indicated. Perforation, massive haemorrhage, or failure to respond to rescue therapy (IV ciclosporin or infliximab) are indications for emergency surgery. Toxic megacolon carries a perforation rate of up to 20% and mortality of 2-5% in modern series. Opioids, anticholinergics, and anti-motility agents are contraindicated as they can worsen dilatation. C. difficile should be excluded (stool sample sent) but the clinical picture here is of acute UC complication.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 75-year-old man presents with a 48-hour history of increasing abdominal distension, vomiting (now faeculent), and absolute constipation. He has lost 6 kg over 3 months. Abdominal X-ray shows grossly dilated large bowel (caecum 10 cm) with a cut-off in the sigmoid region. What is the most concerning feature on the AXR and what is the risk?
Option_A: Caecal dilatation to 10 cm carries a significant risk of perforation and requires urgent decompression
Option_B: Faeculent vomiting indicates the obstruction will resolve spontaneously
Option_C: Large bowel dilatation is normal in elderly patients and requires no urgent action
Option_D: The cut-off at the sigmoid is diagnostic of sigmoid volvulus and can be managed with a flatus tube
Option_E: The weight loss confirms this is metastatic disease and surgery is futile
Explanation: A
Difficulty: The most concerning feature is the caecal dilatation to 10 cm. A caecum diameter >9 cm (some sources cite >12 cm) carries a significant risk of caecal perforation, which is a surgical emergency with high mortality. This patient has a large bowel obstruction (LBO) with features suggestive of a malignant cause: progressive symptoms, weight loss, and a cut-off at the sigmoid (the most common site for colorectal cancer causing obstruction). The 'competent' ileocaecal valve prevents decompression of the obstructed large bowel back into the small bowel, causing progressive caecal distension — a 'closed-loop' obstruction. Management involves urgent surgical referral, IV fluid resuscitation, NBM, and nasogastric tube if vomiting. A CT abdomen with contrast is needed to confirm the site and cause. Emergency surgery (often a Hartmann procedure for left-sided obstructing lesion) or endoscopic stenting as a bridge to surgery may be considered. Sigmoid volvulus has a characteristic 'coffee bean' sign, not a simple cut-off. Weight loss does not preclude surgical intervention.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 52-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presents to the emergency department with jaundice, confusion, and coagulopathy (INR 4.5). She took a staggered overdose of paracetamol over 3 days but did not present until now, 4 days after the first ingestion. Blood tests show: ALT 8,500 U/L, bilirubin 120 μmol/L, creatinine 185 μmol/L, pH 7.28, lactate 5.5 mmol/L. What does this clinical picture represent and what is the most critical action?
Option_A: Acute liver failure — contact the nearest liver transplant centre urgently and commence N-acetylcysteine
Option_B: Chronic liver disease with acute decompensation — start lactulose and rifaximin
Option_C: Drug-induced hepatitis — stop paracetamol and observe
Option_D: Haemolytic jaundice — request a blood film and direct Coombs test
Option_E: This is irreversible and transplantation is contraindicated in overdose patients
Explanation: A
Difficulty: This patient has acute liver failure (ALF): the triad of jaundice, coagulopathy (INR >1.5), and hepatic encephalopathy (confusion) in a patient without pre-existing liver disease, with onset within 26 weeks. Paracetamol overdose is the most common cause of ALF in the UK. The staggered overdose presentation is particularly dangerous because the paracetamol nomogram cannot be used (it is only valid for single acute ingestions with a known time). NICE and the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) recommend that ALL staggered overdoses are treated with N-acetylcysteine (NAC) regardless of paracetamol level. This patient meets King's College Hospital criteria for poor prognosis in paracetamol-induced ALF: arterial pH <7.3 (7.28), AND has INR >6.5 is approaching, creatinine >300 is approaching, with grade III/IV encephalopathy likely developing. The nearest specialist liver transplant centre (e.g., King's College Hospital, Edinburgh, Birmingham) must be contacted urgently. NAC should be commenced and continued. Transplantation is NOT contraindicated in overdose patients — it is a recognised indication for super-urgent liver transplantation.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 68-year-old man with known Crohn disease affecting the terminal ileum presents with a 2-day history of worsening right iliac fossa pain, fever (38.7°C), and a palpable tender mass in the right lower quadrant. A CT abdomen shows a 5 cm inflammatory phlegmon arising from the terminal ileum with no drainable abscess collection. What is the most appropriate initial management?
Option_A: Emergency right hemicolectomy
Option_B: IV antibiotics and IV corticosteroids with close surgical monitoring
Option_C: IV antibiotics alone and plan for delayed surgical resection when inflammation settles
Option_D: Oral antibiotics and discharge with outpatient surgical follow-up
Option_E: Percutaneous drainage of the phlegmon under CT guidance
Explanation: B
Difficulty: This patient has a Crohn disease complication: an inflammatory phlegmon (an inflamed mass of matted bowel and mesentery without a drainable abscess cavity). Management involves: (1) IV antibiotics to treat secondary infection, (2) IV corticosteroids (e.g., hydrocortisone) to treat the active Crohn flare driving the inflammation, (3) bowel rest (NBM/limited oral intake), (4) IV fluids, and (5) close surgical monitoring — because the phlegmon could progress to an abscess or perforation requiring emergency surgery. This is a joint medical-surgical management case. CT-guided percutaneous drainage is appropriate for a well-defined drainable abscess (>3-4 cm) but is not possible with a phlegmon (no drainable fluid collection). Emergency surgery is avoided if possible in active Crohn inflammation because operating on inflamed, friable tissue carries high complication rates and risks of extensive bowel resection. The plan is to settle the acute inflammation with medical therapy and then reassess — elective surgery may be needed later for stricturing/fistulating disease. Discharge on oral antibiotics would be inappropriate given systemic sepsis.

Subject: Gastroenterology
Question: A 35-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset severe epigastric pain that radiates to her back. She has a rigid abdomen with generalised peritonism and is in significant distress. Observations: HR 120 bpm, BP 100/65 mmHg, temperature 37.2°C. An erect chest X-ray shows free gas under the right hemidiaphragm. What does this finding indicate and what is the most appropriate management?
Option_A: Bowel obstruction with secondary perforation — nasogastric tube and IV antibiotics only
Option_B: Perforated hollow viscus — urgent surgical review for emergency laparotomy
Option_C: Pneumomediastinum from oesophageal rupture — arrange urgent CT thorax
Option_D: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy — arrange urgent pelvic ultrasound
Option_E: Spontaneous pneumoperitoneum — this can be observed without surgery
Explanation: B
Difficulty: Free gas (pneumoperitoneum) under the diaphragm on an erect chest X-ray in a patient with peritonism is diagnostic of a perforated hollow viscus until proven otherwise. The most common cause in this age group is a perforated peptic ulcer (duodenal or gastric). This is a surgical emergency requiring: (1) immediate ABC resuscitation (IV access, fluids, analgesia), (2) NBM, (3) nasogastric tube, (4) IV antibiotics (broad-spectrum covering Gram-negatives and anaerobes), (5) urinary catheter, and (6) urgent surgical review for emergency laparotomy (or laparoscopy in some centres) with repair of the perforation (e.g., omental patch repair for a perforated duodenal ulcer). CT abdomen may be performed if the diagnosis is unclear or to help plan the surgical approach, but should not delay surgery in a patient with obvious perforation and peritonism. Spontaneous pneumoperitoneum (without perforation) is exceptionally rare and is a diagnosis of exclusion — it should never be assumed in a patient with peritonism. A ruptured ectopic causes haemoperitoneum, not pneumoperitoneum. Oesophageal rupture (Boerhaave syndrome) typically causes pneumomediastinum rather than subdiaphragmatic free air.

Subject: Endocrinology / Metabolic
Question: A 52-year-old man is diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus. His HbA1c is 62 mmol/mol (target <48). He has a BMI of 31 kg/m² and an eGFR of 85 mL/min. He has no cardiovascular disease. He is started on metformin 500 mg once daily and titrated to 1 g twice daily. After 3 months, his HbA1c remains 60 mmol/mol. According to NICE NG28, what is the most appropriate next step?
Option_A: Add a DPP-4 inhibitor (e.g. sitagliptin)
Option_B: Add a sulfonylurea (e.g. gliclazide)
Option_C: Add an SGLT2 inhibitor (e.g. empagliflozin) or a DPP-4 inhibitor or a sulfonylurea or pioglitazone as dual therapy
Option_D: Start basal insulin immediately
Option_E: Switch metformin to a GLP-1 receptor agonist
Explanation: C
Difficulty: NICE NG28 recommends a stepwise approach to type 2 diabetes management. First-line is metformin (titrated to maximum tolerated dose). If HbA1c remains above the individualised target (typically 48 mmol/mol initially, rising to 53 mmol/mol on dual therapy) after 3-6 months of lifestyle measures plus metformin, the next step is dual therapy: metformin PLUS one of the following — sulfonylurea, DPP-4 inhibitor (gliptin), SGLT2 inhibitor, or pioglitazone. The choice depends on patient factors: SGLT2 inhibitors are preferred if the patient has established cardiovascular disease or heart failure (NICE updated this after the EMPA-REG, CANVAS, and DAPA-HF trials), sulfonylureas if rapid glucose lowering is needed (but carry hypoglycaemia and weight gain risk), DPP-4 inhibitors if hypoglycaemia risk should be minimised, and pioglitazone if insulin resistance is prominent (but contraindicated in heart failure). GLP-1 receptor agonists are considered at the triple therapy stage or if BMI ≥35 with specific conditions. Insulin is not started at this stage unless there is diagnostic uncertainty about type 1 or marked hyperglycaemia with symptoms.

Subject: Endocrinology / Metabolic
Question: A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department with vomiting, abdominal pain, and drowsiness. He has known type 1 diabetes but ran out of insulin 2 days ago. Observations: HR 118 bpm, RR 32 (Kussmaul breathing), BP 95/60 mmHg, temperature 37.1°C. Blood tests show: glucose 35 mmol/L, pH 7.08, bicarbonate 6 mmol/L, potassium 5.8 mmol/L, blood ketones 6.5 mmol/L. What is the correct order of initial management in DKA?
Option_A: Commence fixed-rate insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour immediately — fluids can wait
Option_B: Give 0.9% sodium chloride IV fluid bolus first — then start fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (FRII) at 0.1 units/kg/hour — do NOT give potassium in the first bag as K⁺ is elevated
Option_C: Give insulin bolus subcutaneously followed by oral rehydration
Option_D: Start IV insulin infusion with 5% dextrose as the primary fluid
Option_E: Start potassium replacement immediately before any other treatment
Explanation: B
Difficulty: The Joint British Diabetes Societies (JBDS) DKA protocol provides a clear stepwise approach. The priorities are: (1) IV 0.9% NaCl — start with a rapid 500 mL-1 L bolus over 15-60 minutes depending on haemodynamic status. Fluid resuscitation is the FIRST priority as these patients are severely dehydrated (typically 5-8 L deficit). (2) Start a fixed-rate intravenous insulin infusion (FRII) at 0.1 units/kg/hour AFTER the first fluid bag is commenced. Do NOT give an IV insulin bolus. (3) Potassium replacement: this patient's K⁺ is 5.8 mmol/L (high) — do NOT add potassium to the first bag. However, potassium will fall rapidly once insulin is commenced (insulin drives K⁺ into cells), so K⁺ must be monitored hourly and added to subsequent bags when it falls below 5.5 mmol/L (typically 40 mmol KCl per litre). If K⁺ falls below 3.5, insulin must be withheld until corrected. (4) Monitor glucose, ketones, pH, bicarbonate, and potassium at 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, and 24 hours. (5) Once glucose falls below 14 mmol/L, add 10% dextrose alongside the saline to prevent hypoglycaemia while continuing insulin to clear ketones. Subcutaneous insulin is inadequate in DKA due to poor peripheral perfusion.

Subject: Endocrinology / Metabolic
