Activated charcoal is for oral ingestions within 1 hour. All opioid overdose patients should be offered harm reduction advice, naloxone take-home kit, and referral to drug services on recovery.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 17-year-old girl is referred to the eating disorders service by her GP. She has a BMI of 15 kg/m² (severely underweight), amenorrhoea for 8 months, and admits to restricting food intake, excessive exercise, and distorted body image (she believes she is overweight despite being emaciated). Her heart rate is 42 bpm and she has postural hypotension. Blood tests show: potassium 2.9 mmol/L, phosphate 0.6 mmol/L, albumin 26 g/L, and QTc 490 ms on ECG. According to the MARSIPAN (Management of Really Sick Patients with Anorexia Nervosa) guidelines, which features indicate she is at high risk and requires urgent medical admission?
Option_A: BMI 15 alone is not a medical concern — continue outpatient management
Option_B: Her BMI is low but all other parameters are normal
Option_C: She has multiple high-risk features (BMI <15
Option_D: bradycardia <40 borderline
Option_E: hypokalaemia
Option_G: prolonged QTc) and requires urgent medical admission for stabilisation
Option_H: She only needs admission if she refuses to eat — medical parameters are secondary
Correct_Answer: She should be admitted to a psychiatric ward immediately without medical assessment
column20: This patient has severe anorexia nervosa with multiple medical complications requiring urgent inpatient medical admission. The MARSIPAN guidelines (Royal College of Psychiatrists and Royal College of Physicians) identify medical risk indicators in anorexia nervosa. HIGH-RISK features include: (1) BMI <13 (very high risk) or <15 with rapid weight loss. (2) Heart rate <40 bpm (hers is 42 — borderline). (3) Significant postural hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg). (4) Hypokalaemia <3.0 mmol/L (hers is 2.9 — cardiac risk). (5) Hypophosphataemia (0.6 — refeeding risk and cardiac risk). (6) Prolonged QTc >450 ms (hers is 490 — risk of torsades de pointes and sudden death). (7) Low albumin indicates malnutrition severity. (8) Hypothermia, proximal myopathy, raised transaminases. This patient needs urgent medical admission for: cardiac monitoring (telemetry), cautious electrolyte replacement (especially potassium and phosphate), very cautious refeeding (extremely high risk of refeeding syndrome — start at 5-10 kcal/kg/day per MARSIPAN), and daily monitoring of bloods including phosphate. She will also need psychiatric input but medical stabilisation is the priority. Medical complications of anorexia nervosa are the leading cause of death in any psychiatric illness.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 35-year-old woman with alcohol dependence wishes to stop drinking. She has been drinking 80 units per week for 10 years. She last drank 6 hours ago and is currently asymptomatic. She has no history of withdrawal seizures or delirium tremens. She lives with her partner and has good social support. She does not want to be admitted to hospital. According to NICE CG115, can she undergo community-based (outpatient) detoxification?
Option_A: Community detox is never appropriate — all alcohol detoxification must be inpatient
Option_B: No — she drinks too much for community detox to be safe
Option_C: Yes — community detox can be offered to patients without a history of complicated withdrawal (seizures or DTs) provided they have adequate social support and daily monitoring can be arranged
Option_D: Yes — she can simply stop drinking without any medical support since she is currently asymptomatic
Option_E: Yes — she should be started on disulfiram immediately to aid detoxification
Explanation: C
Difficulty: NICE CG115 (alcohol-use disorders: diagnosis and management) states that community-based assisted withdrawal (detoxification) can be considered for patients who: (1) do NOT have a history of complicated withdrawal (seizures or delirium tremens), (2) do NOT have significant physical or psychiatric comorbidity requiring inpatient management, (3) have adequate social support (someone to supervise them), and (4) can be monitored daily (either by a community alcohol service, GP, or community nurse). This patient meets all criteria. Community detox uses a reducing chlordiazepoxide regimen (same as inpatient) typically over 7-10 days, with daily monitoring of vital signs and withdrawal severity. IV Pabrinex should be given if there is suspected thiamine deficiency. Inpatient detox is indicated for: previous complicated withdrawal, significant medical/psychiatric comorbidity, failed community detox, or high physical dependence without adequate social support. Disulfiram (Antabuse) is used for relapse prevention AFTER detoxification is complete (it causes an unpleasant reaction if alcohol is consumed) — it is NOT used during detoxification. Simply stopping without medical support carries risks of severe withdrawal.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 19-year-old woman with bulimia nervosa presents to the emergency department feeling unwell. She reports binge eating followed by self-induced vomiting up to 8 times daily. On examination, she has swollen parotid glands, calluses on the dorsum of her right hand (Russell sign), and dental erosion. Blood tests show: potassium 2.5 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, chloride 88 mmol/L, bicarbonate 36 mmol/L, urea 12 mmol/L. What is the most dangerous metabolic consequence and how should the hypokalaemia be managed?
Option_A: Hyperkalaemia — give calcium gluconate
Option_B: Hypokalaemia and metabolic alkalosis secondary to recurrent vomiting — urgent IV potassium replacement with cardiac monitoring is required
Option_C: Metabolic acidosis — give IV sodium bicarbonate
Option_D: Respiratory alkalosis — no treatment needed
Option_E: The electrolytes are normal — no treatment required
Explanation: B
Difficulty: Bulimia nervosa is characterised by recurrent binge eating followed by compensatory behaviours (self-induced vomiting, laxative/diuretic misuse, excessive exercise, fasting). The physical signs present here are classic: parotid gland swelling (from repeated vomiting — bilateral), Russell sign (calluses on the dorsum of the hand from inducing vomiting against the upper incisors), and dental erosion (enamel dissolved by gastric acid). The biochemistry shows hypokalaemic hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis — the hallmark of recurrent vomiting. Gastric acid (HCl) loss causes chloride depletion and alkalosis, while potassium is lost both in vomit and through renal compensation (the kidney retains H⁺ and excretes K⁺ to compensate for alkalosis). Potassium of 2.5 mmol/L is dangerously low — this is a medical emergency. Severe hypokalaemia causes: cardiac arrhythmias (including torsades de pointes and cardiac arrest), muscle weakness, paralytic ileus, and rhabdomyolysis. Treatment: IV potassium replacement (maximum rate 20 mmol/hour via central line or 10 mmol/hour via peripheral line) with continuous cardiac monitoring. An ECG should be performed urgently (looking for U waves, flattened T waves, ST depression, prolonged QT).

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 40-year-old man with opioid dependence wishes to start opioid substitution therapy. He has been injecting heroin daily for 5 years. He has no significant liver disease. He is motivated to stop illicit drug use and is engaging with drug services. According to NICE CG51/TA114, what are the first-line options for opioid substitution therapy?
Option_A: Buprenorphine (sublingual) or methadone (oral) — both are recommended first-line options with the choice made collaboratively based on individual factors
Option_B: Codeine phosphate as a substitute for heroin
Option_C: Dihydrocodeine as long-term substitution therapy
Option_D: Naltrexone as first-line substitution
Option_E: Slow-release oral morphine as first-line treatment
Explanation: A
Difficulty: NICE CG51 and TA114 recommend methadone (oral liquid) or buprenorphine (sublingual — e.g., Subutex or the combination product Suboxone which includes naloxone to deter injection) as first-line opioid substitution therapies. The choice depends on patient factors: (1) Methadone: full opioid agonist, given as supervised daily oral liquid (initially). Advantages: once-daily dosing, effective for severe dependence, well-established evidence base. Risks: respiratory depression in overdose, QT prolongation, drug interactions, diversion risk. (2) Buprenorphine: partial opioid agonist, given sublingually. Advantages: lower overdose risk (partial agonist — ceiling effect on respiratory depression), can be prescribed in primary care, available as long-acting depot injection (Buvidal) for improved adherence. Risks: must be started in mild-moderate withdrawal (if given too early, it can precipitate withdrawal by displacing full agonists from receptors). The goal of substitution therapy is to reduce illicit drug use, reduce injecting behaviour (and associated risks — HIV, hepatitis B/C, endocarditis, abscesses), reduce criminal activity, and enable social stabilisation. Naltrexone is an opioid antagonist used for relapse prevention AFTER detoxification, not as substitution. Codeine and dihydrocodeine are not recommended for long-term substitution.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 45-year-old man with schizophrenia is brought to the emergency department by the police. He is acutely psychotic, believes people are trying to poison him, and is refusing all assessment and treatment. He attempted to attack a passer-by before the police intervened. He has been assessed by an approved mental health professional (AMHP) and two doctors (one Section 12 approved). They agree he needs admission for assessment of his mental disorder in the interests of his own health and safety and for the protection of others. Which section of the Mental Health Act 1983 (amended 2007) is most appropriate?
Option_A: Section 135 — warrant to enter premises
Option_B: Section 136 — police power in a public place
Option_C: Section 2 — admission for assessment for up to 28 days
Option_D: Section 3 — admission for treatment for up to 6 months
Option_E: Section 5(2) — doctor's holding power
Explanation: C
Difficulty: Section 2 of the Mental Health Act (MHA) 1983 allows compulsory admission for ASSESSMENT (and treatment necessary for the assessment) for up to 28 days. It is not renewable. Requirements: application by an AMHP (or nearest relative), supported by recommendations from TWO doctors (one must be Section 12 approved — i.e., has special experience in psychiatry). Grounds: the patient is suffering from a mental disorder of a nature or degree warranting detention in hospital for assessment, AND detention is necessary in the interests of the patient's own health or safety or for the protection of other persons. Section 2 is appropriate when: the diagnosis is uncertain, the patient is not previously known to services, or a period of assessment is needed. Section 3 is for TREATMENT (up to 6 months, renewable) when the diagnosis is established and a treatment plan exists. Section 5(2) is a doctor's emergency holding power for a patient ALREADY admitted informally — it lasts 72 hours and allows detention while a full MHA assessment is arranged. Section 136 is a police power to remove a person from a public place to a place of safety — it has already been used here to bring him to the ED. Section 135 authorises entry to private premises.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: An FY2 doctor is on call overnight. A 60-year-old man admitted informally to the medical ward for pneumonia becomes acutely confused and agitated. He is attempting to leave the ward, pulling out his IV cannula, and is at risk of significant harm. He lacks capacity to make decisions about his treatment. The psychiatric team cannot attend for several hours. What power can the FY2 doctor use to prevent the patient from leaving?
Option_A: Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS) — apply immediately and the patient is detained
Option_B: Mental Capacity Act — treat in his best interests using the least restrictive option and if physical restraint or continuous supervision amounts to a deprivation of liberty then an urgent DoLS authorisation should be requested
Option_C: Section 2 of the Mental Health Act — the FY2 can apply this independently
Option_D: Section 5(2) — the doctor's holding power under the Mental Health Act
Option_E: The FY2 has no legal authority to prevent him leaving
Explanation: B
Difficulty: This is a medical patient (pneumonia with delirium) who lacks capacity, NOT a patient with a primary mental disorder requiring psychiatric detention. The Mental Health Act is generally NOT appropriate for patients whose behavioural disturbance is caused by a physical illness (delirium), though this is debated. The correct framework is the Mental Capacity Act 2005 (MCA). Under the MCA: (1) If a person lacks capacity to make a specific decision, treatment can be provided in their BEST INTERESTS. (2) The least restrictive option must always be used. (3) If the level of supervision and control amounts to a deprivation of liberty (continuous supervision, not free to leave, unable to consent), then a Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS) authorisation should be applied for — an urgent authorisation can be granted by the hospital managing authority while a standard authorisation is sought. (4) In an emergency, treatment to prevent serious harm can be given under the MCA without waiting for DoLS. Section 5(2) is a doctor's holding power under the MHA for patients already admitted informally to a PSYCHIATRIC unit or general hospital, but it requires the patient's responsible clinician or nominated deputy — an FY2 on call may use it in some circumstances, but the MCA is the more appropriate framework for a delirious medical patient.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 78-year-old woman with moderate dementia is admitted to hospital with a hip fracture. She is unable to understand, retain, or weigh up information about her treatment and cannot communicate a decision about surgery. Her son insists she would not have wanted surgery. However, the orthopaedic team believes surgery is in her best interests as conservative management carries higher mortality and morbidity. According to the Mental Capacity Act 2005, how should the decision be made?
Option_A: Her son has automatic legal authority to refuse surgery on her behalf as next of kin
Option_B: If she lacks capacity the clinical team must follow the family's wishes regardless
Option_C: The Mental Capacity Act does not apply to surgical decisions
Option_D: The clinical team should make a best interests decision considering all relevant factors — the son's views about what she would have wanted are important but not determinative
Option_E: The consultant can override the MCA in emergency situations without any consultation
Explanation: D
Difficulty: The Mental Capacity Act 2005 provides the legal framework for decisions when a person lacks capacity. The 2-stage capacity test: (1) Is there an impairment of or disturbance in the functioning of the mind or brain? (YES — dementia). (2) Does this impairment mean the person is unable to make the specific decision? This requires ability to understand, retain, use/weigh information, AND communicate a decision. She fails on multiple criteria. When a person lacks capacity, decisions are made in their BEST INTERESTS. The MCA requires consideration of: (1) all relevant circumstances, (2) whether capacity might be regained, (3) the person's past and present wishes, feelings, beliefs, and values, (4) the views of anyone engaged in caring for the person (including family members). Crucially, 'next of kin' has NO automatic legal decision-making authority under the MCA — this is a common misconception. Family views are important and must be consulted, but they are not determinative. Only a person with a valid Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA) for health and welfare, or a court-appointed deputy, has legal authority to make decisions on behalf of someone lacking capacity. If there is a dispute, a best interests meeting should be held, and if unresolved, the Court of Protection can be applied to.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 25-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by police under Section 136 of the Mental Health Act. She was found on a bridge threatening to jump. She is now calm and denies suicidal intent. What is the maximum duration of Section 136 and what must happen during this time?
Option_A: Section 136 allows detention for up to 72 hours — no further assessment is needed if the patient appears better
Option_B: Section 136 has no time limit — the patient can be held until a bed is available
Option_C: Section 136 lasts up to 24 hours (extendable by 12 hours in limited circumstances) — during this time a Mental Health Act assessment must be arranged by an AMHP and a doctor
Option_D: Section 136 lasts up to 7 days and automatically converts to Section 2
Option_E: The patient can be discharged immediately by the police without any medical or psychiatric assessment
Explanation: C
Difficulty: Section 136 of the Mental Health Act 1983 (as amended by the Policing and Crime Act 2017) allows a police officer to remove a person from a public place to a place of safety (usually an ED or designated place of safety suite) if they appear to be suffering from mental disorder AND are in immediate need of care or control. Key provisions: (1) Maximum duration: 24 hours from arrival at the place of safety. This can be extended by up to 12 hours if it is not practicable to complete the assessment within 24 hours (e.g., the person is too intoxicated to be assessed). (2) Purpose: to enable a Mental Health Act assessment by an AMHP and a Section 12 approved doctor. (3) Outcomes of the assessment: the person may be admitted informally (voluntarily), detained under Section 2 or 3 if criteria are met, referred to community mental health services, or discharged if no mental disorder is found or detention is not warranted. (4) A place of safety should NOT be a police cell except in exceptional circumstances. This patient is now calm and denying suicidal intent, but she still requires a proper MHA assessment — she cannot simply be discharged without one. Clinical presentation can fluctuate, and proper risk assessment is essential.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: An FY2 doctor is working on the psychiatric ward. An informal (voluntary) patient with depression who was admitted 3 days ago states he wishes to leave immediately. The nursing staff are concerned because he expressed suicidal ideation this morning. The patient's consultant psychiatrist is not available. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
Option_A: Allow the patient to leave — you cannot detain an informal patient under any circumstances
Option_B: Apply Section 2 of the Mental Health Act immediately — the FY2 can do this alone
Option_C: Contact the patient's consultant or their nominated deputy to consider using Section 5(2) — the doctor's holding power — to detain the patient for up to 72 hours while a full MHA assessment is arranged
Option_D: Physically restrain the patient without any legal authority
Option_E: Sedate the patient with IM haloperidol to prevent him leaving
Explanation: C
Difficulty: Section 5(2) of the Mental Health Act is the doctor's emergency holding power. It allows the doctor in charge of a patient's treatment (the responsible clinician) or their nominated deputy to detain an informal inpatient for up to 72 hours if it appears that an application for compulsory admission (Section 2 or 3) ought to be made. Key points: (1) Only applies to patients who are ALREADY admitted informally to hospital (cannot be used for patients in the ED who have not been formally admitted). (2) Can be exercised by the patient's responsible clinician or their nominated deputy — in practice, the on-call psychiatrist or an approved doctor may act as deputy. An FY2 can potentially use Section 5(2) if they are the nominated deputy. (3) Lasts up to 72 hours — during this time, a formal MHA assessment must be arranged (AMHP + 2 doctors for Section 2, or AMHP + 2 doctors for Section 3). (4) There is also Section 5(4) — a nurse's holding power lasting up to 6 hours, which can be used by a registered mental health nurse if no doctor is immediately available. The FY2 should contact the consultant/deputy, carefully document the clinical concern, and ensure the patient is safe while the assessment is arranged. Informal patients CAN be detained if proper legal processes are followed.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 72-year-old man with atrial fibrillation and hypertension suddenly develops right-sided weakness affecting his face and arm, and expressive dysphasia. His wife calls 999. Paramedics perform a FAST assessment which is positive. They estimate symptom onset was 2 hours ago. He is brought to the emergency department. A CT head shows no haemorrhage. What is the most time-critical treatment?
Option_A: Aspirin 300 mg immediately and admit to the stroke unit
Option_B: IV alteplase (thrombolysis) — the treatment window is within 4.5 hours of symptom onset and this patient is within the window
Option_C: IV heparin infusion as first-line treatment for acute ischaemic stroke
Option_D: Oral clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose
Option_E: Urgent carotid endarterectomy
Explanation: B
Difficulty: This patient has an acute ischaemic stroke (FAST-positive: Face, Arm, Speech, Time) within 2 hours of onset. CT head excludes haemorrhage (essential before thrombolysis). NICE NG128 (stroke and TIA) recommends IV alteplase (recombinant tissue plasminogen activator — rt-PA) for eligible patients with acute ischaemic stroke presenting within 4.5 hours of symptom onset (or time last known well). This is the single most time-critical intervention — 'time is brain'. Eligibility criteria include: confirmed ischaemic stroke on CT, within 4.5 hours, no contraindications (recent surgery, active bleeding, severe hypertension >185/110, anticoagulation with INR >1.7, recent stroke, etc.). The earlier thrombolysis is given, the better the outcome — the number needed to treat (NNT) is approximately 4-5 if given within 90 minutes. For patients with large vessel occlusion, mechanical thrombectomy should also be considered (within 6 hours, or up to 24 hours in selected patients based on perfusion imaging). Aspirin 300 mg is given AFTER thrombolysis (24 hours later) or immediately if thrombolysis is not given. Heparin is NOT used in acute ischaemic stroke. Carotid endarterectomy is for secondary prevention, not acute treatment.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 45-year-old woman presents with the worst headache of her life — sudden onset, reaching maximum intensity within seconds. She describes it as being 'hit on the back of the head with a cricket bat'. She vomited twice and has neck stiffness. She has no focal neurological deficit. A CT head performed within 6 hours of onset is reported as normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
Option_A: CT angiogram immediately — the CT head being normal excludes SAH
Option_B: Discharge with analgesia and GP follow-up — the normal CT excludes SAH
Option_C: Lumbar puncture — performed at least 12 hours after symptom onset — looking for xanthochromia
Option_D: MRI brain as the next investigation
Option_E: Repeat CT head in 24 hours
Explanation: C
Difficulty: This is a classical presentation of suspected subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH): thunderclap headache (sudden-onset, severe, maximal at onset — often described as the worst headache of their life), vomiting, and meningism (neck stiffness from blood irritating the meninges). CT head is the first investigation and is highly sensitive for SAH within 6 hours of onset (approaching 100% sensitivity with modern scanners). However, a NORMAL CT does NOT exclude SAH — sensitivity falls significantly after 6 hours (approximately 93% at 6 hours, 50% at 1 week). If the CT is normal (or equivocal) and clinical suspicion remains, a lumbar puncture (LP) must be performed. Critically, the LP should be performed at least 12 hours after symptom onset — this allows time for red blood cells to break down and produce bilirubin, which causes xanthochromia (yellow discolouration of the CSF supernatant detected by spectrophotometry). Xanthochromia distinguishes true SAH from a traumatic tap. If xanthochromia is present: SAH is confirmed and urgent neurosurgical referral is needed (CT angiogram to identify the aneurysm, followed by coiling or clipping). Discharging this patient without LP would be a dangerous error — missed SAH has a re-bleed rate of up to 40% with high mortality.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 30-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 2-year history of recurrent episodes of unilateral throbbing headache associated with nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia. Episodes last 4-72 hours and she typically needs to lie down in a dark room. She has tried paracetamol which is only partially effective. She has 2-3 attacks per month. What is the most appropriate acute treatment to offer?
Option_A: Codeine 30 mg with paracetamol as first-line
Option_B: Ergotamine as first-line acute treatment
Option_C: Oral triptan (e.g. sumatriptan) with or without an NSAID and an antiemetic
Option_D: Opioid analgesics (tramadol) as first-line
Option_E: Prophylactic treatment only — there is no effective acute treatment for migraine
Explanation: C
Difficulty: This patient has migraine without aura (common migraine): recurrent episodes of unilateral, throbbing headache lasting 4-72 hours with associated nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia, with disability (needs to lie in a dark room). NICE CG150 (headaches) recommends the following acute treatment: (1) First-line: oral triptan (e.g., sumatriptan 50-100 mg) OR an NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen 400 mg, aspirin 900 mg), OR a combination of triptan + NSAID. (2) Add an antiemetic (metoclopramide or prochlorperazine) if nausea/vomiting is present — these also enhance absorption of oral analgesics. (3) Triptans are 5-HT1B/1D receptor agonists that are specifically effective for migraine — they cause cerebral vasoconstriction and inhibit trigeminal nerve activation. Contraindicated in ischaemic heart disease, previous stroke/TIA, uncontrolled hypertension, and basilar or hemiplegic migraine. (4) Paracetamol alone is less effective than NSAIDs or triptans. (5) Opioids (codeine, tramadol) should be AVOIDED — they are associated with medication overuse headache and are less effective. (6) Ergotamine is no longer recommended by NICE due to inferior efficacy and side effects. Prophylaxis should be discussed as she has ≥2 attacks per month.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 35-year-old woman has been diagnosed with episodic migraine. She is having 5 attacks per month despite optimised acute treatment. Her GP considers starting prophylactic medication. According to NICE CG150, which of the following is a first-line prophylactic option?
Option_A: Amitriptyline as an off-label option
Option_B: Gabapentin as first-line prophylaxis
Option_C: Propranolol or topiramate as first-line prophylactic medications
Option_D: Sumatriptan taken daily as prophylaxis
Option_E: Verapamil as first-line for migraine prophylaxis
Explanation: C
Difficulty: NICE CG150 recommends considering migraine prophylaxis when attacks are frequent (typically ≥4 per month), significantly disabling, or inadequately controlled with acute treatment alone. First-line prophylactic options: (1) Propranolol (a non-selective beta-blocker) — typically 40-240 mg daily. Contraindicated in asthma. (2) Topiramate — typically 25-100 mg daily. Important: topiramate is teratogenic (Pregnancy Prevention Programme applies — similar to valproate) and can cause cognitive side effects ('dopamax'), paraesthesia, weight loss, and renal stones. Both have good evidence for reducing migraine frequency. If first-line agents fail or are contraindicated: amitriptyline (off-label, commonly used in practice — 10-75 mg at night), candesartan (off-label), or newer options such as CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab — NICE-approved for chronic migraine after failure of 3 prior prophylactics). Gabapentin is NOT recommended by NICE for migraine prophylaxis (despite some use in clinical practice). Sumatriptan is an acute treatment, not a prophylactic. Verapamil is first-line for cluster headache prophylaxis, not migraine.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 40-year-old man presents to the emergency department with excruciating unilateral pain centred around his right eye. The pain came on rapidly, is described as a 'red-hot poker in the eye', and has been present for 45 minutes. He is restless and pacing the room. On examination, he has right-sided lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion, and partial ptosis. He has had similar attacks daily for the past 3 weeks, always on the right side, typically lasting 1-2 hours. What is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate acute treatment?
Option_A: Acute angle-closure glaucoma — give pilocarpine eye drops
Option_B: Cluster headache — give high-flow oxygen (100% at 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask) and/or subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg
Option_C: Migraine with aura — give oral sumatriptan
Option_D: Temporal arteritis — start prednisolone 60 mg immediately
Option_E: Trigeminal neuralgia — start carbamazepine
Explanation: B
Difficulty: This is a classic cluster headache presentation: excruciating unilateral periorbital pain (often described as the worst pain imaginable), ipsilateral autonomic features (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion, ptosis/miosis — partial Horner syndrome), restlessness/agitation (unlike migraine where patients prefer to lie still), attacks lasting 15 minutes to 3 hours, occurring in clusters (daily attacks for weeks/months then remission). Cluster headache predominantly affects men (3:1 male-to-female ratio). NICE CG150 acute treatment: (1) High-flow 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask at 12-15 L/min for 15-20 minutes — effective in ~70% of attacks. (2) Subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg (or intranasal sumatriptan 20 mg if injection declined) — rapid onset, effective in ~75%. (3) Do NOT use oral triptans — too slow in onset for the short duration of cluster attacks. Prophylaxis during a cluster bout: verapamil (first-line — requires ECG monitoring for heart block), with a short course of prednisolone as a bridging treatment while verapamil takes effect. Trigeminal neuralgia causes brief lancinating pain (seconds) triggered by touch. Temporal arteritis affects older patients with raised ESR/CRP. Glaucoma causes visual disturbance and a fixed mid-dilated pupil.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 68-year-old man presents with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and slurred speech that resolved completely within 45 minutes. He is now entirely back to normal. His blood pressure is 155/92 mmHg and he is in sinus rhythm. He has no previous history of stroke or TIA. His ABCD2 score is 5. What is the most appropriate management according to NICE NG128?
Option_A: Aspirin 300 mg immediately and urgent specialist assessment within 24 hours — with brain imaging and carotid imaging to guide secondary prevention
Option_B: CT head and thrombolysis as for an acute stroke
Option_C: Discharge with GP follow-up in 2 weeks and start aspirin
Option_D: No treatment is needed as symptoms have resolved
Option_E: Warfarin initiation immediately
Explanation: A
Difficulty: This patient has had a transient ischaemic attack (TIA) — a transient episode of neurological dysfunction caused by focal cerebral ischaemia lasting <24 hours (typically minutes to 1 hour) without evidence of acute infarction. NICE NG128 recommends: (1) Aspirin 300 mg IMMEDIATELY (given as soon as TIA is suspected — this can be started by the GP, paramedic, or in the ED). (2) Urgent specialist assessment and investigation within 24 hours of symptom onset — regardless of ABCD2 score (NICE no longer uses ABCD2 to stratify urgency, as it was found to be unreliable at identifying high-risk patients). (3) Brain imaging (MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging preferred, or CT if MRI unavailable) to exclude haemorrhage and identify infarction. (4) Carotid imaging (duplex ultrasound or CT/MR angiography) — if significant carotid stenosis (50-99%) is found, urgent carotid endarterectomy should be considered (within 2 weeks). (5) Secondary prevention: after the initial 300 mg aspirin loading, long-term antiplatelet therapy (clopidogrel 75 mg daily is first-line per NICE), statin (atorvastatin 80 mg — secondary prevention dose), blood pressure management, and lifestyle modification. Thrombolysis is NOT indicated as symptoms have resolved.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 55-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of daily headache. She describes a bilateral, pressing/tightening quality headache that is mild to moderate in intensity. It does not prevent her from working. There is no nausea, photophobia, or phonophobia. She has been taking paracetamol and codeine daily for the past 2 months. Neurological examination is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Chronic migraine
Option_B: Cluster headache
Option_C: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension
Option_D: Medication overuse headache
Option_E: Tension-type headache
Explanation: D
Difficulty: While the headache description (bilateral, pressing, mild-moderate, no associated features) could initially suggest chronic tension-type headache, the critical clue is daily analgesic use for more than 2 months. Medication overuse headache (MOH) — previously called analgesic rebound headache — is defined as headache occurring ≥15 days per month in a patient with a pre-existing headache disorder who has been regularly overusing acute headache medication for >3 months. The overuse thresholds are: simple analgesics (paracetamol, NSAIDs) ≥15 days/month; triptans, opioids, ergotamine, or combination analgesics ≥10 days/month. Codeine-containing analgesics are particularly associated with MOH. NICE CG150 emphasises that MOH should be suspected in any patient with chronic daily headache and regular analgesic use. Management: (1) Explain the diagnosis — the medication is perpetuating the headache. (2) Withdraw the overused medication — this can be done abruptly for simple analgesics/triptans, or gradually for opioids/codeine (to avoid withdrawal symptoms). (3) Warn that headache may initially worsen for 2-4 weeks before improving. (4) Offer prophylactic medication if the underlying primary headache disorder warrants it. (5) Limit future acute medication to <10-15 days per month.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 28-year-old obese woman (BMI 38 kg/m²) presents with a 6-week history of progressive headache, worse on bending forward and in the morning, with transient visual obscurations (brief episodes of greying-out of vision lasting seconds). On examination, fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilloedema. Visual acuity is 6/9 bilaterally. CT head is normal. A lumbar puncture shows an opening pressure of 32 cmH₂O (raised — normal <25) with normal CSF composition. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Brain tumour — arrange urgent MRI with contrast
Option_B: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis
Option_C: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH)
Option_D: Migraine with aura
Option_E: Normal pressure hydrocephalus
Explanation: C
Difficulty: This is a classic presentation of idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH — previously called pseudotumour cerebri or benign intracranial hypertension). The typical patient is a young, obese woman of childbearing age. Key features: (1) Progressive headache (often worse in the morning and with Valsalva manoeuvres/bending forward — raised intracranial pressure). (2) Transient visual obscurations (brief episodes of visual loss lasting seconds, often triggered by posture change — caused by papilloedema compressing the optic nerve). (3) Bilateral papilloedema on fundoscopy. (4) Normal CT/MRI brain (excludes space-occupying lesion, hydrocephalus). (5) Raised CSF opening pressure (>25 cmH₂O in adults) with normal CSF composition (protein, glucose, cells all normal — this distinguishes IIH from meningitis). Associated features: pulsatile tinnitus, sixth nerve palsy (false localising sign from raised ICP). Risk factors: obesity (particularly recent weight gain), tetracycline antibiotics, oral contraceptive pill, vitamin A excess. Management per NICE: (1) Weight loss (the most effective long-term treatment). (2) Acetazolamide (carbonic anhydrase inhibitor — reduces CSF production). (3) Serial visual field monitoring — vision loss is the major morbidity. (4) If vision threatened: CSF diversion (optic nerve sheath fenestration or ventriculoperitoneal shunt). Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis should be excluded with CT/MR venography.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 75-year-old woman with hypertension presents with sudden-onset severe headache, vomiting, and reduced consciousness (GCS 10). On examination, she has a left hemiplegia and her right pupil is fixed and dilated. A CT head shows a large right-sided intracerebral haemorrhage with midline shift. What is the most important initial management priority?
Option_A: Arrange urgent thrombolysis — this will dissolve the clot
Option_B: Commence IV mannitol and urgent neurosurgical referral for consideration of clot evacuation — ABC stabilisation and blood pressure management
Option_C: Give aspirin 300 mg for secondary prevention
Option_D: Start warfarin to prevent further haemorrhage
Option_E: The CT findings are an artefact — repeat the scan
Explanation: B
Difficulty: This patient has a primary intracerebral haemorrhage (ICH) — the second most common type of stroke (~15% of all strokes). Risk factors include hypertension (the most common cause — chronic hypertension causes lipohyalinosis of small penetrating arteries), anticoagulation, cerebral amyloid angiopathy (elderly), and vascular malformations. This is a neurosurgical emergency: large haemorrhage with midline shift and signs of transtentorial herniation (fixed dilated ipsilateral pupil from compression of the third nerve, contralateral hemiplegia). Management: (1) ABC stabilisation — secure airway (GCS 10 — consider intubation), ventilation, circulation. (2) Blood pressure management — NICE NG128 recommends intensive BP lowering (target systolic <140 mmHg within 1 hour using IV labetalol or nicardipine) for ICH within 6 hours if systolic is 150-220 mmHg. (3) Reverse any anticoagulation urgently (if on warfarin: prothrombin complex concentrate + IV vitamin K; if on DOAC: specific reversal agents). (4) IV mannitol or hypertonic saline may reduce cerebral oedema. (5) Urgent neurosurgical referral — evacuation may be considered for superficial haemorrhages with deterioration. (6) Aspirin and thrombolysis are absolutely CONTRAINDICATED in haemorrhagic stroke.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 70-year-old man has an ischaemic stroke affecting the right middle cerebral artery territory. He is thrombolysed within 3 hours and makes a partial recovery. At 48 hours, he has residual left arm and leg weakness and left-sided neglect. According to NICE NG128, what are the key components of ongoing stroke unit care?
Option_A: Bed rest for 2 weeks to allow brain recovery
Option_B: Discharge home after 48 hours with community rehabilitation only
Option_C: Immediate transfer to a general medical ward for ongoing care
Option_D: Multidisciplinary stroke unit care with early rehabilitation — including physiotherapy and occupational therapy and speech therapy as appropriate — plus secondary prevention and screening for complications
Option_E: No further treatment is needed after thrombolysis — recovery is spontaneous
Explanation: D
Difficulty: NICE NG128 and the National Clinical Guideline for Stroke emphasise that ALL stroke patients should be admitted to a dedicated stroke unit — this is one of the most evidence-based interventions in stroke care, reducing mortality and disability regardless of age, sex, or stroke severity. Stroke unit care includes: (1) Multidisciplinary team: stroke physicians, specialist nurses, physiotherapists, occupational therapists, speech and language therapists, dietitians, psychologists, and social workers. (2) Early rehabilitation: should begin as soon as the patient is medically stable (typically within 24-48 hours). Early mobilisation, swallowing assessment (dysphagia screen before oral intake to prevent aspiration pneumonia), continence management, and mood assessment. (3) Secondary prevention: antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 300 mg for 2 weeks then clopidogrel 75 mg long-term), atorvastatin 80 mg, blood pressure management, anticoagulation for AF (after 2 weeks), lifestyle modification. (4) Screening for complications: DVT/PE prophylaxis, pressure sore prevention, nutrition, depression (common post-stroke), cognitive assessment. (5) Discharge planning and community rehabilitation. Transfer to a general medical ward is associated with worse outcomes. Bed rest is harmful — early mobilisation improves recovery.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 76-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation has an ischaemic stroke. She was not on anticoagulation prior to the stroke. Her CHADS-VASc score is 5 (age, female, hypertension, diabetes, stroke). After the acute phase, when should anticoagulation be started for secondary prevention according to NICE NG128?
Option_A: Anticoagulation should be started immediately on day 1
Option_B: Anticoagulation should never be started after a stroke — use aspirin lifelong
Option_C: Start anticoagulation at 2 weeks after an ischaemic stroke (or sooner for TIA) — a DOAC (e.g. apixaban or edoxaban) is preferred over warfarin
Option_D: Start anticoagulation at 6 months after stroke
Option_E: Start warfarin immediately with bridging heparin
Explanation: C
Difficulty: For patients with AF and ischaemic stroke, anticoagulation for secondary prevention is essential but should be delayed to reduce the risk of haemorrhagic transformation of the infarct. NICE NG128 recommends: (1) Start anticoagulation at approximately 2 weeks after an ischaemic stroke (earlier if the infarct is small and haemorrhagic transformation has been excluded on repeat imaging). (2) For TIA with AF: anticoagulation can be started sooner (after brain imaging excludes haemorrhage). (3) A DOAC (direct oral anticoagulant) is preferred over warfarin: apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, or dabigatran. DOACs have comparable or superior efficacy, lower risk of intracranial haemorrhage, and do not require routine INR monitoring. Apixaban and edoxaban are commonly used. (4) In the interim (before anticoagulation starts), aspirin 300 mg daily is given for the first 2 weeks for its antiplatelet effect. (5) This patient's CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of 5 means her annual stroke risk without anticoagulation is approximately 6-7% — anticoagulation is strongly indicated. Starting anticoagulation immediately (day 1) carries a risk of haemorrhagic transformation. Aspirin alone is inferior to anticoagulation for stroke prevention in AF.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 50-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 3-week history of new-onset headache, jaw claudication (pain on chewing), tenderness over her right temple, and general malaise. She has also noticed blurring of vision in her right eye over the past 2 days. Blood tests show: ESR 95 mm/hr and CRP 68 mg/L. What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most critical immediate action?
Option_A: Migraine — start propranolol prophylaxis
Option_B: Temporomandibular joint dysfunction — refer to dental services
Option_C: Tension-type headache — prescribe paracetamol
Option_D: This is giant cell arteritis (temporal arteritis) — start high-dose prednisolone (40-60 mg daily) IMMEDIATELY to prevent irreversible visual loss — do not wait for biopsy results
Option_E: Trigeminal neuralgia — start carbamazepine
Explanation: D
Difficulty: This is giant cell arteritis (GCA/temporal arteritis) — a large vessel vasculitis affecting branches of the external carotid artery (particularly the temporal artery). The key features are: new-onset headache in a patient >50, temporal artery tenderness, jaw claudication (pain on chewing — due to ischaemia of the masseter muscles), constitutional symptoms (malaise, fatigue, weight loss), markedly raised ESR/CRP, and — critically — visual symptoms (blurring, amaurosis fugax, or sudden visual loss from anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy). GCA is an ophthalmological emergency because untreated GCA causes irreversible bilateral blindness in up to 20% of patients. IMMEDIATE high-dose prednisolone (40-60 mg daily for uncomplicated GCA, or IV methylprednisolone 500 mg-1 g daily for 3 days if visual symptoms are present) must be started on clinical suspicion BEFORE biopsy results. Temporal artery biopsy (TAB) should be arranged within 2 weeks of starting steroids (histological changes persist for several weeks after steroid initiation, so starting treatment does not negate biopsy). BSR/BHPR guidelines recommend adding low-dose aspirin. Steroids are tapered very slowly over 1-2 years guided by symptoms and inflammatory markers.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: An FY2 doctor is called to the ward where a 55-year-old man has been having a continuous generalised tonic-clonic seizure for 8 minutes. He has known epilepsy. The nurse has placed him in the recovery position, protected his airway, and given high-flow oxygen. Buccal midazolam 10 mg was given 5 minutes ago but the seizure continues. IV access has now been obtained. What is the next step in the status epilepticus protocol?
Option_A: Give a second dose of buccal midazolam
Option_B: IV lorazepam 4 mg given over 2 minutes — repeat once after 10 minutes if seizure continues
Option_C: IV phenytoin immediately without trying lorazepam
Option_D: Observe for another 20 minutes before any further treatment
Option_E: Rectal diazepam 10 mg as it is faster than IV treatment
Explanation: B
Difficulty: Status epilepticus is defined as a seizure lasting >5 minutes (or two or more seizures without full recovery of consciousness between them). It is a medical emergency with significant mortality if untreated. The stepwise protocol (NICE CG137 / Advanced Life Support): STEP 1 (0-5 minutes): ABC, oxygen, recovery position, check blood glucose. Give buccal midazolam 10 mg or rectal diazepam 10 mg (pre-hospital/no IV access). STEP 2 (5-15 minutes — first-line failed): IV lorazepam 4 mg over 2 minutes. Can be repeated ONCE after 10 minutes if seizure continues. Lorazepam is preferred IV because it has a longer duration of anticonvulsant action than diazepam and less respiratory depression. STEP 3 (>25 minutes — second-line failed): This is refractory status epilepticus. Options include IV phenytoin (or fosphenytoin) 20 mg/kg as a loading dose with cardiac monitoring (risk of arrhythmias and hypotension), IV levetiracetam, or IV sodium valproate. STEP 4: If still seizing, this is super-refractory — requires general anaesthesia (propofol or thiopental) with ICU transfer. This patient has failed buccal midazolam — the next step is IV lorazepam 4 mg. A second dose of buccal midazolam should not be given if IV access is available.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 22-year-old woman has a witnessed first tonic-clonic seizure at work. She has no previous history of epilepsy, no head injury, and no family history. By the time paramedics arrive the seizure has stopped and she is in the post-ictal phase. On arrival at the emergency department she is alert and orientated with a normal neurological examination. Blood tests including glucose, calcium, sodium, and renal function are normal. What investigations should be arranged and what driving advice must be given?
Option_A: CT head and EEG as an inpatient today — she can continue driving as this was a single seizure
Option_B: Discharge with no follow-up — a single seizure does not require investigation
Option_C: MRI brain and EEG arranged as an outpatient via first seizure clinic referral — she must stop driving and notify the DVLA
Option_D: No imaging is needed — start an antiepileptic drug immediately
Option_E: Urgent lumbar puncture to exclude meningitis
Explanation: C
Difficulty: NICE CG137 (epilepsies) recommends that ALL patients with a first seizure should be referred to a specialist (first seizure clinic or epilepsy service) for assessment within 2 weeks. Investigations: (1) MRI brain — the preferred imaging modality (superior to CT for detecting structural causes such as tumours, cortical dysplasia, mesial temporal sclerosis, vascular malformations). CT head is used acutely if there is concern about acute intracranial pathology. (2) EEG — to help classify the seizure type and epilepsy syndrome. Neither investigation needs to be done as an emergency if the patient has recovered and examination is normal. (3) Blood tests: FBC, U&Es, glucose, calcium, liver function, and prolactin (if taken within 20 minutes of the seizure — may support a diagnosis of generalised tonic-clonic seizure). DRIVING: the patient MUST be informed of DVLA regulations. For a Group 1 licence (car/motorcycle): she must stop driving and notify the DVLA. After a single unprovoked seizure, she cannot drive for 6 months (if no cause found and investigations normal) or 12 months (if abnormalities found). Antiepileptic drugs are NOT started after a single seizure unless there is a high risk of recurrence — the decision is made by a specialist.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 25-year-old woman with juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (JME) presents to her GP for a medication review. She is well controlled on sodium valproate 500 mg BD and seizure-free for 3 years. She mentions she is in a new relationship and thinking about starting a family in the next year. What is the most critical prescribing action?
Option_A: Continue sodium valproate throughout pregnancy — it is the most effective drug for JME
Option_B: No changes are needed — valproate is safe in pregnancy at this dose
Option_C: She must be counselled about the significant teratogenic risks of valproate and switched to a safer alternative (e.g. levetiracetam or lamotrigine) BEFORE conception — under specialist supervision
Option_D: Stop valproate immediately without starting an alternative — the risk of seizures is less important than teratogenicity
Option_E: Switch to carbamazepine as it is the safest AED in pregnancy
Explanation: C
Difficulty: This is one of the most important prescribing safety issues in medicine. Sodium valproate is a MAJOR teratogen: up to 10% risk of major congenital malformations (neural tube defects, cardiac defects, facial clefts) and 30-40% risk of neurodevelopmental disorders (reduced IQ, autism spectrum disorder). The MHRA Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PREVENT) mandates that valproate must NOT be prescribed to women of childbearing potential unless they are on effective contraception and enrolled in the PPP. For this patient who is planning pregnancy: (1) Refer urgently to her epilepsy specialist. (2) Switch valproate to a safer alternative — for JME, levetiracetam or lamotrigine are reasonable alternatives (though lamotrigine may be less effective for the myoclonic seizures of JME). (3) The switch must be done BEFORE conception, gradually, under specialist supervision — abrupt withdrawal risks breakthrough seizures and status epilepticus. (4) Start high-dose folic acid (5 mg daily) before conception and continue through the first trimester. (5) Carbamazepine can worsen JME (a generalised epilepsy) and is primarily used for focal epilepsies. (6) Simply stopping valproate without an alternative puts her at risk of uncontrolled seizures (also dangerous in pregnancy).

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 60-year-old man presents to his GP after a witnessed episode where he suddenly stopped talking mid-sentence, stared blankly, made lip-smacking and hand-fumbling movements for about 90 seconds, and then appeared confused for 5 minutes before returning to normal. He has no recollection of the event. This is the third such episode in 2 months. What type of seizure does this describe?
Option_A: Absence seizure (petit mal)
Option_B: Focal seizure with impaired awareness (previously called complex partial seizure)
Option_C: Generalised tonic-clonic seizure
Option_D: Non-epileptic attack (psychogenic)
Option_E: Simple partial seizure
Explanation: B
Difficulty: This is a focal seizure with impaired awareness (previously termed complex partial seizure). The key features are: (1) Impaired awareness/consciousness during the event (he is unresponsive and has no memory of it). (2) Automatisms — repetitive, semi-purposeful movements (lip-smacking, hand-fumbling/picking at clothes). (3) Duration of 60-120 seconds (longer than typical absence seizures). (4) Post-ictal confusion (5 minutes of confusion afterwards). (5) No preceding aura described here, though many patients have one (e.g., epigastric rising sensation, déjà vu, olfactory hallucination — suggesting temporal lobe origin). Focal seizures arise from a localised area of the brain (most commonly the temporal lobe). They are classified as: focal aware (consciousness preserved — previously simple partial), focal with impaired awareness (consciousness impaired — previously complex partial), or focal to bilateral tonic-clonic (secondary generalisation). Absence seizures (petit mal) occur in children/adolescents, are very brief (5-20 seconds), involve sudden blank staring with immediate recovery and NO post-ictal confusion. Generalised tonic-clonic seizures involve whole-body stiffening and rhythmic jerking. The first-line AED for focal seizures is lamotrigine or levetiracetam (NICE CG137).

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 30-year-old man with newly diagnosed focal epilepsy is started on lamotrigine. He is counselled about a specific serious adverse effect that requires immediate medical attention. What is this reaction and what must he be told?
Option_A: Aplastic anaemia — check FBC weekly
Option_B: Hepatotoxicity — check LFTs monthly
Option_C: Pancreatitis — avoid alcohol completely
Option_D: Renal toxicity — check U&Es weekly
Option_E: Serious skin rash (Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis) — report any new rash immediately especially in the first 8 weeks
Explanation: E
Difficulty: Lamotrigine is associated with serious skin reactions: Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) and toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN). These are rare (approximately 1 in 1,000-10,000) but potentially fatal immune-mediated reactions causing widespread epidermal necrosis, mucosal involvement, and multiorgan dysfunction. Risk is highest in the first 8 weeks of treatment and is increased by: (1) Rapid dose escalation — lamotrigine MUST be titrated slowly (starting at 25 mg daily, increasing every 2 weeks). (2) Concurrent use of valproate — valproate inhibits lamotrigine metabolism, increasing levels and rash risk. If co-prescribed, lamotrigine must be started at an even lower dose (12.5 mg or 25 mg on alternate days). Patients must be counselled: (1) Report ANY new rash, mouth ulcers, blistering, or skin peeling immediately. (2) Seek urgent medical assessment. (3) Stop lamotrigine if a serious rash is suspected (until a specialist has reviewed — do not restart). Most lamotrigine-associated rashes are benign, but it is impossible to distinguish early SJS from a benign rash clinically, so all rashes must be taken seriously. The slow titration schedule is critical and must not be accelerated. Aplastic anaemia is associated with carbamazepine. Hepatotoxicity and pancreatitis are associated with valproate.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 40-year-old woman with well-controlled epilepsy on levetiracetam has been seizure-free for 4 years. She holds a Group 1 (car) driving licence. She asks her GP about the current DVLA driving regulations for epilepsy. Which of the following statements is correct for Group 1 drivers?
Option_A: She can drive as soon as she is started on an antiepileptic drug regardless of seizure control
Option_B: She can hold a Group 1 licence as she has been seizure-free for at least 12 months — she must notify the DVLA of her diagnosis
Option_C: She can never drive again after a diagnosis of epilepsy
Option_D: She must be seizure-free for 5 years before she can drive
Option_E: She only needs to inform the DVLA if she has more than 10 seizures per year
Explanation: B
Difficulty: Current DVLA regulations for epilepsy and Group 1 (car/motorcycle) driving: (1) The patient MUST notify the DVLA of their diagnosis of epilepsy (failure to do so is a criminal offence). (2) To hold a Group 1 licence, they must have been seizure-free for at least 12 months (whether on medication or not). (3) If seizures occur only during sleep (nocturnal epilepsy), the patient may drive after a pattern of sleep-only seizures over 12 months (even if still having sleep seizures, provided no awake seizures for 12 months). (4) If antiepileptic medication is being withdrawn, the DVLA advises not driving during withdrawal and for 6 months after the last dose. (5) Group 2 (lorry/bus) driving: stricter rules — must be seizure-free for at least 10 years AND off all AEDs for at least 10 years. This patient is seizure-free for 4 years and meets the 12-month seizure-free requirement. She can hold a Group 1 licence but must notify the DVLA. The licence is typically issued for a limited period (1-3 years) subject to medical review.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: An FY2 doctor is asked to prescribe phenytoin for a patient in refractory status epilepticus (seizure ongoing despite two doses of IV lorazepam). The patient weighs 70 kg. What is the correct loading dose and what is the most important monitoring requirement during the infusion?
Option_A: Phenytoin 10 mg/kg IV — no monitoring required
Option_B: Phenytoin 15-20 mg/kg IV (e.g. 1000-1400 mg for 70 kg) infused at a maximum rate of 50 mg/min with continuous cardiac monitoring and blood pressure monitoring — risk of arrhythmias and hypotension
Option_C: Phenytoin 20 mg/kg as a rapid IV bolus over 1 minute
Option_D: Phenytoin 5 mg/kg IV — monitoring of blood glucose only
Option_E: Phenytoin should be given intramuscularly for faster absorption
Explanation: B
Difficulty: IV phenytoin is used in refractory status epilepticus when benzodiazepines have failed (Step 3 of the protocol). Prescribing details: (1) Loading dose: 15-20 mg/kg IV (for a 70 kg patient: 1,050-1,400 mg). (2) Maximum infusion rate: 50 mg/min in adults (slower in elderly or those with cardiac disease — 25 mg/min). Fosphenytoin (a phenytoin prodrug) can be infused faster (150 mg phenytoin equivalents/min) and is preferred if available. (3) CRITICAL monitoring: continuous ECG monitoring (cardiac telemetry) and blood pressure monitoring during and for 30 minutes after the infusion. Phenytoin can cause: QT prolongation, bradycardia, heart block, ventricular arrhythmias, and severe hypotension. If arrhythmia or hypotension occurs, the infusion rate must be slowed or stopped. (4) Phenytoin must be given through a large vein with 0.9% NaCl flush — it is highly alkaline (pH 12) and causes severe tissue necrosis if extravasated (purple glove syndrome). It is incompatible with dextrose solutions. (5) Phenytoin should NEVER be given as a rapid bolus — this causes cardiac arrest. (6) IM phenytoin is contraindicated — erratic absorption and causes muscle necrosis. Alternative third-line agents include IV levetiracetam or IV sodium valproate.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of blurred vision in her right eye, pain on eye movement, and reduced colour vision. On examination, her visual acuity is 6/36 in the right eye and 6/6 in the left. There is a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) on the right. Fundoscopy is normal. She had an episode of tingling and numbness in both legs 18 months ago that resolved spontaneously over 6 weeks. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute angle-closure glaucoma
Option_B: Anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy
Option_C: Multiple sclerosis presenting with optic neuritis
Option_D: Retinal detachment
Option_E: Vitamin B12 deficiency optic neuropathy
Explanation: C
Difficulty: This is optic neuritis — inflammation of the optic nerve — a common presenting feature of multiple sclerosis (MS). Key features: (1) Unilateral visual loss developing over hours to days (NOT sudden — sudden visual loss suggests vascular causes). (2) Pain on eye movement (85% of cases). (3) Reduced colour vision (dyschromatopsia) — often the earliest and most sensitive finding. (4) Relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD/Marcus Gunn pupil) — indicates asymmetric optic nerve dysfunction. (5) Fundoscopy may be NORMAL in retrobulbar optic neuritis (the most common type — 'the doctor sees nothing and the patient sees nothing') or may show optic disc swelling in anterior optic neuritis. The previous episode of sensory symptoms in both legs (likely a spinal cord demyelinating lesion) makes MS the most likely unifying diagnosis — two clinical episodes disseminated in time and space (optic nerve + spinal cord). MS is diagnosed using the McDonald criteria: evidence of CNS lesions disseminated in time and space, supported by MRI brain and spinal cord (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord lesions), CSF (oligoclonal bands present in >95% of MS), and visual evoked potentials. Referral to neurology for urgent MRI is essential.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 65-year-old man presents with a 12-month history of progressive resting tremor in his right hand, slowness of movement, and difficulty initiating walking. His wife notes he has become stooped and his facial expression has reduced. On examination, he has a 4-6 Hz pill-rolling resting tremor of the right hand, cogwheel rigidity in the right arm, and bradykinesia (slow finger-tapping with decrementing amplitude). His gait is shuffling with reduced arm swing on the right. What is the most likely diagnosis and the first-line treatment?
Option_A: Benign essential tremor — propranolol
Option_B: Drug-induced parkinsonism — stop the causative medication
Option_C: Normal pressure hydrocephalus — arrange lumbar puncture
Option_D: Parkinson disease — levodopa (co-beneldopa or co-careldopa) is the most effective symptomatic treatment
Option_E: Progressive supranuclear palsy — no treatment available
Explanation: D
Difficulty: This is a classic presentation of idiopathic Parkinson disease: the cardinal motor features are (1) resting tremor (typically 4-6 Hz, pill-rolling, asymmetric, worse at rest and reduced with action), (2) rigidity (cogwheel or lead-pipe), (3) bradykinesia (slowness of movement with decrementing amplitude — this is the defining feature required for diagnosis), and (4) postural instability (develops later). Asymmetric onset is characteristic. Non-motor features include anosmia, REM sleep behaviour disorder, constipation, depression, and cognitive decline. Diagnosis is clinical (no diagnostic test). NICE NG71 (Parkinson disease): (1) Refer to a specialist with expertise in PD (neurologist or movement disorder specialist) before starting treatment. (2) First-line treatment: levodopa (combined with a decarboxylase inhibitor — co-beneldopa or co-careldopa) is the most effective symptomatic treatment for motor symptoms. (3) Dopamine agonists (ropinirole, pramipexole, rotigotine) or MAO-B inhibitors (rasagiline, selegiline) may be used as initial therapy or adjuncts, particularly in younger patients to delay levodopa-related motor complications. (4) Essential tremor causes an ACTION tremor (worse with movement), not resting tremor. Drug-induced parkinsonism (from antipsychotics) should be excluded.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 55-year-old man presents with progressive weakness of his right hand and foot drop over 6 months. He has fasciculations visible in his thigh and shoulder muscles. He has no sensory loss. On examination: upper limb wasting (particularly the small muscles of the right hand), hyperreflexia in the lower limbs, upgoing plantars bilaterally, and tongue fasciculations. His cognition is preserved. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Cervical spondylotic myelopathy
Option_B: Guillain-Barré syndrome
Option_C: Motor neurone disease (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis)
Option_D: Multiple sclerosis
Option_E: Myasthenia gravis
Explanation: C
Difficulty: This is motor neurone disease (MND), specifically amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) — the most common form. The hallmark is COMBINED upper and lower motor neurone signs WITHOUT sensory involvement: Lower motor neurone (LMN) signs: weakness, wasting (small muscles of the hand — 'claw hand'), fasciculations (visible muscle twitching, including tongue fasciculations — highly suggestive of MND). Upper motor neurone (UMN) signs: hyperreflexia, upgoing plantars (positive Babinski), spasticity. The absence of sensory loss is critical — MND is a PURE motor disease (if sensory loss is present, consider alternative diagnoses). Tongue fasciculations suggest bulbar involvement. The El Escorial criteria require: (1) evidence of LMN degeneration, (2) evidence of UMN degeneration, (3) progressive spread of signs within a region or to other regions, (4) absence of other explanations. Prognosis is poor (median survival 2-5 years from symptom onset). Riluzole is the only disease-modifying drug (extends survival by approximately 3 months). Management is multidisciplinary (respiratory support — NIV, nutrition — PEG, communication aids, palliative care). Guillain-Barré is acute, ascending, and has areflexia. Myasthenia causes fatigable weakness without wasting or fasciculations. MS includes sensory symptoms.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 35-year-old man presents with a 5-day history of progressive ascending weakness starting in his feet and now affecting his legs and hands. He has paraesthesia in his fingers and toes. He had a gastroenteritis illness (Campylobacter) 3 weeks ago. On examination: flaccid weakness (MRC grade 3/5 in legs, 4/5 in arms), areflexia throughout, and no sensory level. His respiratory rate is 22 and he is unable to count to 20 in a single breath. What is the most important investigation to perform NOW and what is the treatment?
Option_A: CT head to exclude intracranial pathology
Option_B: Forced vital capacity (FVC) measurement urgently — if FVC <20 mL/kg or falling the patient needs ICU transfer — treatment is IV immunoglobulin (IVIg) or plasma exchange
Option_C: Lumbar puncture immediately as the only necessary investigation
Option_D: MRI spine to exclude cord compression — no other treatment is needed
Option_E: Nerve conduction studies before any treatment
Explanation: B
Difficulty: This is Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) — an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy. Classic features: (1) Progressive, symmetrical, ascending weakness (legs → arms → respiratory and bulbar muscles). (2) Areflexia/hyporeflexia. (3) Post-infectious (2-4 weeks after Campylobacter jejuni — most common trigger — or other GI/respiratory infections, or rarely vaccination). (4) Sensory symptoms (paraesthesia) are common but objective sensory loss is typically mild. The MOST CRITICAL assessment is respiratory function — GBS can cause respiratory failure. Forced vital capacity (FVC) must be measured urgently and regularly (every 4-6 hours). Criteria for ICU transfer/intubation: FVC <20 mL/kg (or <1 L), FVC declining >30% from baseline, inability to count to 20 in a single breath, bulbar dysfunction with aspiration risk. This patient has respiratory involvement (RR 22, cannot count to 20) — he needs urgent ICU assessment. Treatment: (1) IV immunoglobulin (IVIg) 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days — first-line, OR plasma exchange (equally effective). (2) Corticosteroids are NOT effective in GBS. Supportive care: VTE prophylaxis, pain management (neuropathic pain is common), physiotherapy. Nerve conduction studies and LP (raised CSF protein with normal cell count — albuminocytological dissociation) support the diagnosis but must not delay treatment or respiratory assessment.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 19-year-old university student presents with headache, fever (39.5°C), neck stiffness, photophobia, and a non-blanching petechial rash over his trunk and limbs. He is drowsy (GCS 13). His observations show: HR 120, BP 95/55, RR 24. What is the most likely diagnosis and the most critical immediate action?
Option_A: Encephalitis — give IV aciclovir immediately
Option_B: Meningococcal meningitis/septicaemia — give IV ceftriaxone 2 g IMMEDIATELY without waiting for any investigation
Option_C: Migraine with rash — give sumatriptan and paracetamol
Option_D: Subarachnoid haemorrhage — arrange CT head first
Option_E: Viral meningitis — reassure and discharge with oral analgesia
Explanation: B
Difficulty: This is a medical emergency — meningococcal meningitis/septicaemia (Neisseria meningitidis). The triad of headache, fever, and neck stiffness with a non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash is meningococcal disease until proven otherwise. The patient is septic (tachycardia, hypotension, tachypnoea) and has reduced consciousness. NICE CG102 and the Meningitis Research Foundation guidance are clear: IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone 2 g IV, or benzylpenicillin if ceftriaxone is unavailable) must be given IMMEDIATELY — do NOT delay for CT, LP, or blood cultures if there is clinical suspicion. Every minute of delay increases mortality. In the community (pre-hospital): IM benzylpenicillin should be given by the GP before transfer. Further management: (1) Aggressive fluid resuscitation. (2) Blood cultures, blood glucose, FBC, CRP, lactate, coagulation, and meningococcal PCR should be taken BUT must not delay antibiotics. (3) LP should be deferred if there are signs of raised intracranial pressure, coagulopathy, haemodynamic instability, or extensive rash. (4) Consider dexamethasone 10 mg IV (given before or with the first dose of antibiotics — reduces mortality and complications in bacterial meningitis, particularly pneumococcal). (5) Close contacts need chemoprophylaxis (ciprofloxacin single dose or rifampicin for 2 days). (6) Notify Public Health England.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 70-year-old woman with Parkinson disease on co-careldopa (levodopa/carbidopa) has been well controlled for 5 years. Over the past 6 months she has developed 'wearing-off' phenomenon — her symptoms return predictably before her next dose is due. She also experiences involuntary writhing movements (dyskinesia) at peak dose. What are these complications and how should they be managed?
Option_A: Increase the levodopa dose to control the wearing-off — the dyskinesia is unrelated
Option_B: Stop levodopa entirely and switch to a dopamine agonist alone
Option_C: The wearing-off and dyskinesia are motor complications of long-term levodopa therapy — options include adding a COMT inhibitor (entacapone) or MAO-B inhibitor to extend levodopa effect and adjusting doses to reduce peak-dose dyskinesia
Option_D: These symptoms indicate she does not have Parkinson disease — reconsider the diagnosis
Option_E: This is a normal part of ageing and no treatment changes are needed
Explanation: C
Difficulty: Wearing-off (end-of-dose deterioration) and peak-dose dyskinesia are common motor complications of long-term levodopa therapy, typically emerging after 5-10 years. As the disease progresses, the therapeutic window narrows — the dose needed to control motor symptoms gets closer to the dose that causes dyskinesia. (1) Wearing-off: symptoms return before the next levodopa dose (the on-off fluctuations). Management: add a COMT inhibitor (entacapone — inhibits peripheral levodopa metabolism, prolonging its action) to each levodopa dose, or add a MAO-B inhibitor (rasagiline or safonamide — reduces central dopamine breakdown), or increase the frequency of levodopa doses (smaller, more frequent doses), or use controlled-release levodopa. (2) Peak-dose dyskinesia: involuntary choreiform/writhing movements at the time of peak levodopa plasma level. Management: reduce individual levodopa doses (with more frequent dosing to compensate), or add amantadine (NMDA receptor antagonist with anti-dyskinetic properties). (3) For refractory motor fluctuations: deep brain stimulation (DBS — targeting the subthalamic nucleus or globus pallidus interna) or levodopa-carbidopa intestinal gel (LCIG) may be considered. NICE NG71 recommends specialist review for motor complications.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 30-year-old woman presents with intermittent double vision, drooping eyelids that worsen as the day progresses, and difficulty swallowing. She finds that her arms become weak when blow-drying her hair. On examination, she has bilateral ptosis that worsens on sustained upward gaze and fatigable proximal weakness in the upper limbs. Reflexes and sensation are normal. What is the most likely diagnosis and what investigation would confirm it?
Option_A: Guillain-Barré syndrome — nerve conduction studies
Option_B: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome — voltage-gated calcium channel antibodies
Option_C: Motor neurone disease — EMG
Option_D: Myasthenia gravis — anti-acetylcholine receptor (AChR) antibodies and/or repetitive nerve stimulation
Option_E: Thyroid eye disease — thyroid function tests
Explanation: D
Difficulty: This is myasthenia gravis (MG) — an autoimmune condition where antibodies (most commonly anti-AChR antibodies — positive in ~85% of generalised MG) target the post-synaptic acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction. Key features: (1) FATIGABLE weakness — symptoms worsen with repeated use and improve with rest. (2) Ocular involvement: ptosis and diplopia are often the earliest features (50% present with ocular symptoms). (3) Bulbar involvement: dysphonia, dysphagia, difficulty chewing. (4) Proximal limb weakness. (5) Symptoms characteristically worsen towards the end of the day. (6) Normal reflexes and sensation (neuromuscular junction disease, not nerve or muscle disease). Investigations: (1) Anti-AChR antibodies (positive in ~85% generalised, ~50% ocular-only). (2) Anti-MuSK antibodies (in AChR-negative patients). (3) Repetitive nerve stimulation — shows decremental response. (4) CT chest — to exclude thymoma (present in ~10-15% of MG patients; thymic hyperplasia in ~60%). (5) Edrophonium (Tensilon) test — rarely used now due to cardiac risks. Treatment: (1) Pyridostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) — symptomatic. (2) Immunosuppression (prednisolone, azathioprine) for generalised disease. (3) Thymectomy. (4) Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure): IVIg or plasma exchange.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 50-year-old man presents with a 2-day history of progressive facial weakness on the right side. He is unable to close his right eye, raise his right eyebrow, or smile on the right side. The rest of his neurological examination is entirely normal. He has no ear pain, vesicles, hearing loss, or limb weakness. What is the most likely diagnosis and the recommended treatment?
Option_A: Bell palsy — prescribe prednisolone 50 mg daily for 10 days started within 72 hours of onset
Option_B: Cerebral stroke affecting the facial nerve — arrange urgent CT head and thrombolysis
Option_C: Lyme disease — start doxycycline without further investigation
Option_D: Parotid gland tumour compressing the facial nerve — arrange urgent MRI
Option_E: Ramsay Hunt syndrome — start aciclovir and prednisolone
Explanation: A
Difficulty: This is Bell palsy — an idiopathic lower motor neurone (LMN) facial nerve (VII) palsy. Key features distinguishing LMN from UMN facial weakness: LMN palsy affects ALL muscles on the ipsilateral side (forehead included — cannot raise eyebrow or close eye), whereas UMN palsy (e.g., stroke) spares the forehead (because the forehead receives bilateral UMN innervation). This patient has complete unilateral facial weakness including the forehead — LMN pattern. The absence of vesicles excludes Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster reactivation in the geniculate ganglion — causes facial palsy, severe ear pain, and vesicles in the ear canal/pinna; treated with aciclovir + prednisolone). NICE CKS and evidence-based guidelines recommend: (1) Prednisolone 50 mg daily for 10 days (or 60 mg for 5 days then taper over 5 days), started within 72 hours of symptom onset — this improves recovery rates significantly. (2) Eye care: artificial tears, eye taping at night (to prevent corneal exposure and drying — the eye cannot close). (3) Antiviral agents (aciclovir) are NOT recommended routinely for Bell palsy (only for Ramsay Hunt syndrome). (4) Prognosis: 85-90% make a full recovery. (5) Consider Lyme disease (bilateral facial palsy in endemic areas) and exclude other causes.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: An 82-year-old woman is admitted to the surgical ward following a hip fracture. On post-operative day 2, the nursing staff report that she has become acutely confused, agitated, and is seeing 'people in the room who are not there'. She was orientated and cognitively normal on admission. Her observations: temperature 38.2°C, HR 100, BP 130/75, SpO2 94% on air, urine output reduced. A 4AT score is 8 (positive for delirium). What is the most important next step in management?
Option_A: Administer haloperidol 5 mg IM to sedate her immediately
Option_B: Assume she has dementia and arrange a memory clinic referral
Option_C: Discharge her home as the confusion will resolve spontaneously
Option_D: Prescribe lorazepam 2 mg regularly to keep her calm
Option_E: Systematically identify and treat the underlying cause — check for infection (urine dipstick and cultures plus blood cultures and CXR) plus review medications plus assess pain plus check constipation and urinary retention plus correct dehydration and electrolytes
Explanation: E
Difficulty: This is acute delirium — an acute confusional state characterised by acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention, and either altered consciousness or disorganised thinking. The 4AT (Assessment Test for Delirium) is a rapid screening tool recommended by NICE and SIGN: scores ≥4 suggest possible delirium. The CRITICAL management principle is to identify and treat the underlying CAUSE — delirium is always a symptom of an underlying precipitant in a vulnerable patient. Common precipitants (mnemonic PINCH ME): Pain, Infection (UTI and chest infection are the most common — this patient is febrile), Nutrition/dehydration, Constipation, Hypoxia, Medication (opioids, benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, steroids), Environment (unfamiliar surroundings, sleep deprivation). Investigation: urinalysis/culture, blood cultures, CXR, FBC, CRP, U&Es (dehydration, AKI, electrolyte disturbance), calcium, glucose, LFTs, medication review, bladder scan (retention). Management of the delirium itself: (1) Non-pharmacological first — reorientation, adequate lighting, familiar objects, consistent carers, clock/calendar, promote sleep, mobilise, correct sensory impairments (glasses/hearing aids).
