(2) Antipsychotics (haloperidol 0.5-1 mg — note the LOW dose) are reserved for patients who are a danger to themselves or others and only after non-pharmacological measures fail. High-dose sedation is dangerous.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A GP is assessing a 75-year-old woman whose family have noticed progressive memory problems over 18 months. She forgets recent conversations, repeats questions, and has got lost in familiar places. She is managing her personal care but her husband does all the cooking and finances now. Her mood is normal. On examination, there are no focal neurological signs. An Addenbrooke Cognitive Examination (ACE-III) scores 62/100 (below threshold). Blood tests including FBC, U&Es, calcium, glucose, TFTs, B12, folate, and LFTs are normal. An MRI brain shows medial temporal lobe (hippocampal) atrophy with no vascular changes. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Alzheimer disease
Option_B: Depression (pseudodementia)
Option_C: Frontotemporal dementia
Option_D: Lewy body dementia
Option_E: Vascular dementia
Explanation: A
Difficulty: This is a classic presentation of Alzheimer disease (AD) — the most common cause of dementia (~60-70% of cases). Key features: (1) Gradual, progressive onset with insidious memory impairment as the earliest and most prominent feature (episodic memory — forgetting recent events and conversations, repeating). (2) Later: visuospatial difficulties (getting lost), language problems (word-finding), executive dysfunction, apraxia. (3) MRI: medial temporal lobe/hippocampal atrophy (the hippocampus is critical for memory formation). (4) Normal blood tests exclude reversible causes of cognitive decline. Distinguishing features of other dementias: (1) Vascular dementia: stepwise deterioration, executive dysfunction predominant, vascular risk factors, white matter changes/lacunar infarcts on MRI. (2) Lewy body dementia: visual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, parkinsonism, REM sleep behaviour disorder, severe sensitivity to antipsychotics. (3) Frontotemporal dementia: personality and behavioural changes BEFORE memory loss, disinhibition, apathy, language problems, frontal/temporal atrophy on MRI, younger onset (45-65). (4) Depression (pseudodementia): low mood, subjective memory complaints often exceed objective deficits, responds to antidepressant treatment. NICE NG97 recommends cognitive assessment, blood screen to exclude reversible causes, and structural brain imaging.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 72-year-old man is brought to his GP by his wife. She is concerned because over the past year he has had vivid visual hallucinations (seeing children and animals in the house), fluctuating alertness (some days he is lucid, other days profoundly confused), and he has developed a shuffling gait with stiffness. He also acts out his dreams violently at night (REM sleep behaviour disorder). His memory is relatively preserved compared to his visuospatial function (he can no longer navigate or judge distances). What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Alzheimer disease
Option_B: Charles Bonnet syndrome
Option_C: Delirium
Option_D: Lewy body dementia
Option_E: Parkinson disease dementia
Explanation: D
Difficulty: This is dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) — the second most common neurodegenerative dementia after Alzheimer disease. The revised McKeith criteria for DLB identify core clinical features: (1) Fluctuating cognition with pronounced variations in attention and alertness. (2) Recurrent, well-formed visual hallucinations (typically of people, children, or animals — often detailed and non-threatening). (3) REM sleep behaviour disorder (acting out dreams — may precede cognitive symptoms by years). (4) Spontaneous features of parkinsonism (bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor — though tremor may be less prominent than in PD). All four are present in this patient. Cognitive profile: visuospatial and attentional deficits are prominent early (unlike AD where memory is the earliest deficit). CRITICAL prescribing safety: patients with DLB have severe neuroleptic sensitivity — antipsychotics (even atypical ones) can cause life-threatening reactions (severe rigidity, obtundation, autonomic instability) and are relatively contraindicated. If antipsychotic treatment is absolutely necessary, low-dose quetiapine or clozapine may be considered with extreme caution. Treatment: acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil or rivastigmine — NICE-approved for DLB, can help hallucinations and cognitive fluctuations). Parkinson disease dementia is the same pathology but PD motor symptoms precede dementia by >1 year (the '1-year rule').

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 78-year-old man with a diagnosis of mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease is reviewed in the memory clinic. His cognitive function has been declining gradually over 2 years. He is living at home with support from his wife. According to NICE NG97, what is the first-line pharmacological treatment?
Option_A: Donepezil (or rivastigmine or galantamine) — an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor
Option_B: Ginkgo biloba supplement
Option_C: Memantine as first-line for mild-to-moderate disease
Option_D: No pharmacological treatment is available or recommended
Option_E: Prednisolone to reduce neuroinflammation
Explanation: A
Difficulty: NICE NG97 (dementia) recommends acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors as first-line pharmacological treatment for mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease. The three options are: (1) Donepezil — most commonly used, once daily, generally well tolerated. (2) Rivastigmine — available as oral or transdermal patch (patch has fewer GI side effects). (3) Galantamine — also has nicotinic receptor modulation. These drugs inhibit acetylcholinesterase, increasing acetylcholine levels in the brain (compensating for the cholinergic deficit in AD). They provide modest symptomatic benefit — stabilising or slowing cognitive decline for 6-12 months, and may improve behavioural symptoms. Common side effects: nausea, diarrhoea, insomnia, bradycardia (caution in cardiac conduction disease). For moderate-to-severe Alzheimer disease: memantine (an NMDA receptor antagonist) can be used as monotherapy or added to an AChE inhibitor. Memantine is NOT first-line for mild disease. Non-pharmacological interventions are equally important: cognitive stimulation therapy (CST), structured group activities, exercise, and carer support. AChE inhibitors are NOT recommended for mild cognitive impairment or frontotemporal dementia. They ARE recommended for DLB and Parkinson disease dementia.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: An FY2 doctor is asked to assess a 78-year-old woman on the medical ward who has been confused since admission 3 days ago with a urinary tract infection. She has a background of mild Alzheimer disease but was at her baseline cognitive function before admission. The nurse asks whether she has delirium or worsening dementia. Which feature most reliably distinguishes delirium from dementia?
Option_A: Delirium has an acute onset with a fluctuating course — dementia has a gradual onset with a progressive course
Option_B: Delirium always causes visual hallucinations while dementia never does
Option_C: Delirium only occurs in patients without pre-existing cognitive impairment
Option_D: Dementia causes acute confusion while delirium causes chronic confusion
Option_E: There is no reliable way to distinguish delirium from dementia
Explanation: A
Difficulty: Distinguishing delirium from dementia is a fundamental clinical skill. The key differentiating features are: DELIRIUM: (1) ACUTE onset (hours to days). (2) FLUCTUATING course (lucid intervals alternating with confusion). (3) Impaired attention (the hallmark — patient cannot focus, is easily distractible). (4) Usually caused by an identifiable precipitant (infection, medication, metabolic disturbance). (5) Potentially REVERSIBLE if the cause is treated. (6) Altered level of consciousness (may be hyperactive/agitated or hypoactive/drowsy — hypoactive delirium is often missed). DEMENTIA: (1) GRADUAL onset (months to years). (2) PROGRESSIVE course (steadily worsening). (3) Memory impairment is typically the earliest feature. (4) Attention is relatively preserved until late stages. (5) Consciousness is usually clear (alert). (6) Irreversible (though symptoms may fluctuate in DLB). CRITICAL: delirium and dementia frequently COEXIST — pre-existing dementia is the single strongest risk factor for developing delirium. Delirium superimposed on dementia is very common in hospitalised elderly patients (this patient has both). The presence of pre-existing dementia does not exclude delirium — both must be assessed and delirium actively treated. The 4AT tool screens for delirium regardless of baseline cognitive status.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 68-year-old man presents to the memory clinic with a 2-year history of personality change. His wife reports he has become socially disinhibited (making inappropriate sexual comments to strangers), apathetic (lost interest in his hobbies), and has developed compulsive eating behaviours (consuming large quantities of sweet foods). His memory is relatively preserved. On examination, he has primitive reflexes (grasp reflex). An MRI brain shows bilateral frontal and anterior temporal lobe atrophy. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Alzheimer disease
Option_B: Bipolar disorder — manic episode
Option_C: Frontotemporal dementia (behavioural variant)
Option_D: Huntington disease
Option_E: Normal pressure hydrocephalus
Explanation: C
Difficulty: This is behavioural variant frontotemporal dementia (bvFTD) — the most common form of frontotemporal dementia. Key distinguishing features: (1) Personality and behavioural changes are the EARLIEST and most prominent features — BEFORE memory decline. This is the key distinction from Alzheimer disease. (2) Disinhibition (socially inappropriate behaviour, loss of manners, impulsive actions). (3) Apathy and loss of empathy. (4) Compulsive/ritualistic behaviours and dietary changes (particularly sweet food craving — 'sweet tooth'). (5) Executive dysfunction. (6) Relative preservation of memory and visuospatial function (initially). (7) Younger onset than AD — typically 45-65 years. (8) MRI: frontal and/or anterior temporal lobe atrophy (the 'knife-edge' atrophy pattern). (9) Primitive reflexes (grasp, palmomental, pout) indicate frontal lobe dysfunction. There are no effective pharmacological treatments for bvFTD — management is supportive (behavioural strategies, carer support, environmental modification, SALT assessment for swallowing difficulties as disease progresses). AChE inhibitors are NOT recommended (may worsen behavioural symptoms). The other main FTD variants are semantic dementia and progressive non-fluent aphasia (language-predominant forms). Bipolar mania is episodic, not progressive.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: An 85-year-old woman with advanced dementia is admitted to hospital with pneumonia. She is treated with IV antibiotics and recovers from the infection. However, her family notice that her cognitive function has deteriorated significantly compared with her pre-admission baseline and has not returned to normal after 3 weeks. What does this represent and what should the family be counselled about?
Option_A: She has developed a new stroke — arrange CT head
Option_B: She was misdiagnosed with dementia — this is purely delirium and she will recover fully
Option_C: The cognitive deterioration is temporary and will fully reverse once the infection has resolved
Option_D: This likely represents a step-change decline in her dementia trajectory accelerated by the delirium — delirium in dementia patients is associated with faster long-term cognitive decline and the family should be counselled that she may not return to her previous baseline
Option_E: This represents normal hospital deconditioning and requires only physiotherapy
Explanation: D
Difficulty: This is an important and commonly encountered scenario. Delirium superimposed on dementia is extremely common in hospitalised elderly patients and has significant prognostic implications. Evidence shows: (1) Delirium accelerates the trajectory of cognitive decline in patients with pre-existing dementia. (2) Following an episode of delirium, many patients with dementia do NOT return to their pre-delirium cognitive baseline — there is a permanent 'step-change' decline. (3) Delirium in dementia patients is associated with increased risk of: faster progression to severe dementia, increased mortality (up to 2-fold increased risk), prolonged hospital stay, and increased likelihood of institutionalisation. (4) This does not mean recovery is impossible — some patients do improve, but often not to their previous level. The family should be counselled sensitively: (1) Explain that the infection-related delirium has likely caused a step-change in her dementia. (2) She may improve further with time, rehabilitation, and return to a familiar environment, but may not return to her previous level. (3) Discuss ongoing care needs, advance care planning, and support for carers. (4) This is an appropriate time to involve palliative care if not already involved. (5) Ensure comprehensive discharge planning with community support.
